text:
In the labyrinthine alleys of our modern metropolises, where the staccato rhythm of urban life echoes off graffiti-laden walls, there exists an unlikely luminary. Shrouded in secrecy, this enigmatic figure, known only as Banksy, has ignited a furor that transcends geographical boundaries and cultural barriers - a phenomenon so potent it defies simple categorization. At once rebellious and subversive, poetic and political, Banksy's street art serves as a mirror reflecting the complexities of our era, particularly the intricate web of power structures and capitalism that bind us all.

Consider, if you will, the surprising contradiction at the heart of this discourse: a multi-millionaire anonymously creating art to critique wealth accumulation and social inequality. This enigma, in itself, is a testament to the very system it seeks to challenge - one that elevates those who question its foundations above their less fortunate peers. It's as if Banksy's own success serves as a cautionary tale, a vivid reminder of the fickle nature of power and the capriciousness of fortune in our capitalist society.

Banksy's art is a Rorschach test, inviting viewers to project their own interpretations upon his images. In one such masterpiece, "Girl with Balloon," a young girl reaches for a heart-shaped balloon escaping the confines of a frame - an image that speaks volumes about the fleeting nature of hope in the face of oppressive forces. But it is not only symbolism that Banksy employs to challenge power structures; his works also often employ biting satire, such as in "Capitalist Pigs," where he depicts a pig wearing a suit and tie, standing on its hind legs and reading a newspaper - a commentary on the commodification of greed and excess.

But Banksy's critique extends beyond capitalism itself, delving into the very fabric of power structures that underpin our society. In "Flower Thrower," we see an individual hurling flowers rather than weapons, suggesting that even nonviolent resistance can be powerful in the face of oppression. Similarly, his work "Cut it Out" depicts a police officer cutting through the Union Jack flag, symbolizing the dismantling of national identity as a means of control. These pieces remind us that power structures, though seemingly immutable, are ultimately fragile and dependent on our complicity.

As I stood before Banksy's "Slave Labour" mural in London, I couldn't help but feel the weight of his message pressing down upon me. The image depicted a young boy meticulously painting a red logo onto a pair of sneakers, while another child watches with longing eyes - an unmistakable critique of the exploitative labor practices that fuel the capitalist machine. The irony was palpable; here I stood, a privileged bystander, marveling at this piece of art created by an unknown artist who, in turn, sought to expose the very system that had granted me my own privilege.

As I walked away from Banksy's mural, I couldn't help but feel a sense of unease - a nagging awareness that his works served as both a mirror and a warning, reflecting our world back at us in all its ugliness and beauty while urging us to consider the role we play in perpetuating the very systems he seeks to dismantle. Banksy's art, like the capitalist system it critiques, is ever-evolving, constantly adapting to the changing landscape of our society. And as long as there are those who dare to question and resist, there will always be room for his art - and for hope.

Consider the poignant brushstrokes of a grief-stricken Van Gogh, or the poignant notes of a melancholic Beethoven's piano sonata. These creative offerings are not mere aesthetic pleasures; they are windows into the heart of the artist, reflecting their joy, pain, and all shades in between. Through these windows, we find solace, understanding, and even a sense of shared humanity.

Yet, it's not just about reflection - artistic expression influences our behavior as well. It stirs within us emotions we may have forgotten, questions we've been too afraid to ask, or dreams we've suppressed. A powerful piece of art can inspire acts of compassion, provoke thoughtful discourse, or challenge the status quo.

Take, for instance, the groundbreaking works of Frida Kahlo or Pablo Picasso. Their bold depictions of reality dared to shatter societal norms and traditional assumptions about gender, politics, and identity. They compelled us to question, reconsider, and ultimately, grow.

But isn't it intriguing how art can both reflect and shape our world? It's a fascinating paradox - a mirror that shows us who we are while also molding who we could become. Artistic expression, in its essence, is a conversation between the artist and the viewer, a dialogue that transcends time and space.

So, as we stand before these masterpieces, engrossed in their stories, let's not just marvel at the beauty but ponder upon the lessons they hold, the questions they ask, and the truths they reveal. For art is not merely an expression of human emotion; it's a journey into our collective consciousness, a testament to our shared humanity, and a beacon that guides us towards understanding ourselves better.

And as we delve deeper, let's remember to question, to explore, to empathize - for the greatest works of art are not just pretty pictures or melodious symphonies; they are conversations waiting to happen, stories longing to be told, and truths yearning to be revealed.

Title: An Unexpected Delight at Steakhouse Haven on a Busy Weekday! 

Oh my, folks, let me tell you about my recent visit to this gem of a steakhouse tucked away in the bustling city heart. I was a bit wary given its reputation for being crowded, especially during weekdays, but boy, was it worth it!

Stepping into Steakhouse Haven was like entering an oasis of culinary elegance amidst the urban jungle. The dimly lit interior, adorned with luxurious wood and sleek leather, exuded a warm, inviting ambiance that instantly put me at ease. My eyes were drawn to the well-dressed patrons enjoying their meals, adding to the vibrant atmosphere.

Now, I'm not one for long waits, but I was pleasantly surprised when they managed to seat me promptly despite the crowd. They even offered outdoor seating, which turned out to be a perfect choice given the pleasant evening weather. The gentle hum of conversation and clinking glasses created a symphony that danced in my ears as I perused their extensive menu.

When it came to choosing my meal, I opted for their signature ribeye steak, cooked to medium-rare perfection. Accompanied by a side of creamy mashed potatoes and seasonal vegetables, the dish was a symphony of flavors that danced on my tongue. Each bite was tender, juicy, and incredibly satisfying.

The service throughout my meal was impeccable - attentive yet unobtrusive, ensuring my glass never went empty while allowing me to savor each morsel without distraction. The staff seemed genuinely passionate about their work, making the dining experience all the more enjoyable.

In closing, if you're a steak aficionado like myself and don't mind the occasional crowd (or even enjoy it!), Steakhouse Haven is a must-visit. Its exceptional cuisine, attentive service, and versatile atmosphere make it an ideal spot for both intimate dinners or lively gatherings with friends. Just remember to book in advance if you want that coveted outdoor seating! Enjoy your steak adventure!

Title: A Quiet Weekday Dinner at Culver's - Solid, but Could Use Some Improvements

Rating: 

Hey Yelpers!

Let me share with you my recent experience at our local Culver's during a quiet weekday evening. I was on a tight budget, so I opted for takeout to save some extra cash.

First things first, the service was excellent! The cashier and the staff were friendly, and they even offered suggestions on their special menu items that fit within my price range. I ended up getting the ButterBurger Classic with a side of Fresh Cut Fries and a small Creamy Lemonade.

The food arrived in a sturdy paper bag, which kept everything warm and fresh during the short drive home. I appreciated the quality of the packaging since it meant that my leftovers would stay fresh for another meal.

Now, let's talk about the taste. The ButterBurger was delicious! It had just the right amount of seasoning, and the butter-grilled patty was perfectly cooked to my preference (medium). The Fresh Cut Fries were crispy on the outside and soft on the inside - a perfect balance! However, I found the Creamy Lemonade a tad too sweet for my taste.

But here's the slight tangent: as I was eating, I couldn't help but notice that the ButterBurger seemed to lack that wow factor. Maybe it was because I had high expectations after hearing so many great things about Culver's, or maybe it was just an off day for them.

Overall, I would say that my experience at Culver's was solid, but not outstanding. The service was friendly and efficient, the food was well-packaged, and most of the items were tasty. However, if you're looking for a restaurant with a mind-blowing burger, you might want to look elsewhere.

So, my fellow budget-conscious Yelpers, I recommend Culver's as a good option for a quick, affordable meal. Just keep in mind that the food may not always live up to your expectations. Until next time, happy dining!

text:
In the grand tapestry of human existence, we find ourselves entangled in a labyrinthine web of intellectual traditions, each thread woven from the fabric of our collective wisdom. These threads, once spun by the minds of great thinkers who came before us, now form the scaffolding upon which we construct our modern understanding of the world. Yet, as we tread the path that leads from the past to the present, we are struck by an unsettling paradox - a contradiction that lies at the heart of our nature as thinking beings.

Consider, if you will, the intellectual traditions that have shaped our modern thought. From the ancient Greeks who grappled with questions of existence and purpose, to the Enlightenment philosophers who championed reason and progress, each tradition has bequeathed to us a legacy of ideas that have molded our understanding of ourselves and our place in the universe. And yet, as we embrace these traditions, we find ourselves confronting the very same questions that confounded their creators - questions that seem to defy resolution, questions that challenge our most cherished beliefs about who we are and what we value.

Take, for example, the tradition of rationalism, which holds that reason is the highest faculty of mankind, capable of unlocking the secrets of the universe. This tradition, born in the crucible of the Enlightenment, promised to us a world illuminated by the light of reason, a world where ignorance would be vanquished and progress would reign supreme. And yet, as we stand on the precipice of this rationalist utopia, we are confronted with the specter of irrationality - the irrationality that lies at the heart of our own nature, the irrationality that refuses to be vanquished by the light of reason.

In the face of this contradiction, we find ourselves asking: How can it be that the very faculty that promises to lead us out of darkness is also the source of our greatest confusion and despair? Is it not strange that the same minds that gave us calculus and the theory of relativity are also capable of constructing elaborate theories about the divine right of kings, or the inherent superiority of one race over another? And if reason is indeed capable of such contradictions, what hope do we have of using it to guide us toward a better future?

These questions, which seem to defy resolution, are but one manifestation of the contradiction that lies at the heart of our intellectual traditions. For, as much as these traditions have shaped our understanding of the world, they have also served to obscure the truths that lie hidden beneath their surface. They have given us a lens through which to view the world, but that lens is tinted by the biases and assumptions of those who came before us. And so, as we gaze upon the world through this lens, we are unable to see it as it truly is - a world of complexity and contradiction, a world where reason and irrationality coexist in an uneasy alliance.

This contradiction, which lies at the heart of our intellectual traditions, serves to remind us that our understanding of the world is always imperfect, always subject to revision. It reminds us that we must continually question our beliefs, continually challenge our assumptions, if we hope to arrive at a deeper and more truthful understanding of the world. And it reminds us that, as much as we may aspire to be guided by reason, we must also recognize and embrace the irrationality that lies within us - for it is only by acknowledging this irrationality that we can hope to transcend it.

In the end, then, our intellectual traditions serve not as a blueprint for the future, but as a compass to guide us on our journey through the labyrinth of human existence. They provide us with a framework for understanding the world, but they also remind us that this understanding is always provisional, always subject to revision. And so, as we navigate the twists and turns of this labyrinth, we must hold fast to the threads of our intellectual traditions, even as we are reminded that these threads are fragile, tenuous things - capable of unraveling at any moment, revealing the darkness that lies beneath.

As we stand on the precipice of a new age, where the power of reason threatens to reshape the very fabric of our world, we must remember the contradictions that lie at the heart of our intellectual traditions. We must remember that, as much as we may aspire to be guided by reason, we are also capable of being led astray by it. And we must remember that, if we hope to create a better future, we must not only embrace the light of reason, but also the darkness of irrationality - for it is only by acknowledging this darkness that we can hope to transcend it.

In this way, our intellectual traditions serve as a reminder that our understanding of the world is always incomplete, always subject to revision. They remind us that we must continually question our beliefs, continually challenge our assumptions, if we hope to arrive at a deeper and more truthful understanding of the world. And they remind us that, as much as we may aspire to be guided by reason, we must also recognize and embrace the irrationality that lies within us - for it is only by acknowledging this irrationality that we can hope to transcend it.

As we move forward into an uncertain future, let us remember these lessons. Let us remember the contradictions that lie at the heart of our intellectual traditions, and let us strive to be guided not only by reason, but also by a deep and abiding humility - a humility that recognizes the limits of our understanding, and the need for continual revision and correction. For it is only by embracing this humility that we can hope to create a better future - a future where reason and irrationality coexist in harmony, where we are able to see the world as it truly is, and where we can work together to build a brighter tomorrow.

text:
In the heart of a bustling city, I stumbled upon an old man tending to his flowers in a small park. His face was etched with lines of age and pain, yet his eyes sparkled with a resilience that only those who've faced adversity can possess. He reminded me of so many others I've met over the years - survivors of conflicts that have torn societies apart.

The concept of justice and forgiveness in post-conflict societies is a complex tapestry, woven with threads of suffering, loss, and hope. Justice, often sought as a means to bring closure and accountability, can at times be a double-edged sword. It can provide a semblance of peace to the victims, but it can also fuel further resentment and division within communities.

Forgiveness, on the other hand, is a delicate dance between healing and reconciliation. It's not about forgetting or condoning the wrongs committed, but rather about finding a way to move forward despite them. Forgiveness is not always easy, especially when the wounds are fresh, and it takes courage and strength to take that step.

I remember visiting Rwanda several years ago, a country still grappling with the aftermath of its genocide. I met survivors who had lost everything - their families, their homes, their dignity. And yet, they spoke of forgiveness not as a choice, but as a necessity for their own healing and that of their nation.

But what about justice? Isn't it important to hold those responsible accountable? Absolutely. However, the pursuit of justice must be balanced with the need for reconciliation and peace. The old man in the park was a testament to this balance. He had lost his family during the conflict, yet he chose to forgive those who had taken them away. He chose to plant flowers, not out of forgetfulness, but as a symbol of hope and new beginnings.

In post-conflict societies, justice and forgiveness are not mutually exclusive, but intertwined paths towards healing and peace. They require courage, patience, and understanding - qualities that the old man in the park exemplified so profoundly. As we navigate these complex ideas, let us remember that every story is unique, every heart heals at its own pace, and every flower blooms in its own time.

Title: A Quirky Quickie at Taco Truck Del Sol 

Star Rating:  / 5

Ah, the magic of a hole-in-the-wall taco truck! That's exactly what I stumbled upon before catching a movie one evening - Taco Truck Del Sol. My vegetarian heart was on tenterhooks as I approached, hoping they'd have something for me beyond the usual beans and rice.

The place was buzzing with energy, a delightful mix of locals and curious passersby. The truck itself was a colorful spectacle, vibrant murals adorning its exterior, adding to the allure. I squeezed my way through the crowd, eager to satisfy my cravings.

I was greeted by a friendly face behind the counter who, with a warm smile, presented me with the menu. My eyes scanned the options, and to my pleasant surprise, there it was - the Al Pastor Verde! A vegan take on the traditional Al Pastor, made from cactus and bell peppers. I paired it with a refreshing Horchata, and boy, was I in for a treat!

The taco was a visual delight - vibrant green hues and aromatic spices teasing my senses. Each bite was a symphony of flavors, the smoky cactus melding beautifully with the sweetness of the bell peppers. The tortilla held it all together, warm and flexible, giving way under the gentle pressure of my teeth.

However, the Horchata, oh the Horchata! It was rich and creamy, yet delicately sweet, with just the right amount of cinnamon to make me feel like I was walking down a Mexican street at sunset. But then came the contradiction - or rather, the inconsistency. One sip and I tasted an underlying bitterness that dampened my experience. It wasn't unbearable, but it did leave me feeling a touch disappointed.

The service was speedy, which was much appreciated given my tight schedule, and the overall ambiance was lively and inviting. So, while the Horchata left something to be desired, I'd definitely visit Taco Truck Del Sol again - perhaps for their famous Burritos de Frijoles if they live up to the hype. If you find yourself in need of a quick, flavorful bite before catching a flick, give this little gem a try!

Title: A Soapy Surprise on a Busy Weekday at Joe's Car Wash 

I gotta admit, I was skeptical when I pulled up to Joe's Car Wash on a bustling weekday. As a grease-loving eater, the thought of someone scrubbing away the remnants of my late-night burger binges didn't exactly fill me with joy. But, I was in desperate need of a good cleanse, so here I was - ready to be won over or left disappointed.

The pricing seemed reasonable enough; $15 for an exterior wash and $20 if you wanted the works. For someone as budget-conscious as myself, it wasn't too steep, but still, I couldn't help feeling a twinge of unease. As I waited in line behind a sea of dirty cars, I could almost smell the tension in the air - everyone eager for that freshly washed feeling, yet hesitant to part with their hard-earned cash.

As my turn approached, I peered through the dirty windows and caught sight of the interior - sparkling clean and well-organized. The attendants wore matching uniforms, and there was a sense of pride in the way they worked. That gave me a glimmer of hope that maybe, just maybe, this car wash could deliver.

The attendant took my payment with a friendly smile and assured me it would be worth it. I watched as he meticulously scrubbed every inch of my car, taking special care around the wheel rims - something I'd always missed in previous washes. It's the little things, right? And boy, did this place have them in spades.

After a few minutes of elbow grease and suds, it was time for the undercarriage wash. Now, I'm not one to get too fussed about what's going on underneath my car, but even I could see the difference as the dirty water streamed away. It was like watching the gunky secrets of my life being washed down the drain - a cathartic experience, if you will.

Once the exterior was spotless, it was time for the interior detailing. As someone with a nut allergy, I'm extra sensitive to the lingering scents in cars - even my own. So I braced myself for any potential allergen triggers. But to my pleasant surprise, the scent that wafted through my open windows was fresh and inviting, with none of the usual chemical smells that often accompany a car wash.

The final touch was the vacuuming, and my car emerged looking brand new - shiny, spotless, and feeling like it had just rolled off the showroom floor. As I drove away, I couldn't help but feel a strange sense of pride in my spick-and-span vehicle. Joe's Car Wash had won me over with their attention to detail, reasonable pricing, and allergy-friendly approach.

In conclusion, if you find yourself in need of a good scrubdown on a busy weekday, give Joe's Car Wash a try. It may not be the cheapest option out there, but the value for money is undeniable - especially when you factor in their commitment to quality and customer satisfaction. Just remember: even grease-loving eaters need a little TLC every now and then!

For Derrida, the idea of the secret is intimately bound up with the idea of ‘confession’, be it political, personal or religious. Confession need not be forced, of course. In fact, it can be an act of love. If I confess to you my ‘deepest secrets’, I am – in commonsense belief – ‘telling you who I really am’, or rather showing you who I really am, by performing a kind of revelation that makes me vulnerable and open, and inviting a reciprocal openness. I trust/love you so much that I have no secrets. More prosaically, it can also be an act of profound friendship – another of Derrida’s themes – and of bonding. When someone says: ‘Do you want to know a secret?’ we feel not only the thrill of the illicit, but a sense of being entered into a community, even if it is one of only two people. We share secrets. In each of these examples, confessions forced or otherwise, the tacit understanding is that the confession is indeed an act of revealing the self. In Nineteen Eighty-Four, Winston exposes his secret and destroys his ‘self’. If I confess to you my secrets, you enter into my most intimate selfhood, we share it. Thus, we have a commonsense – and legalistic – belief that there is a direct relationship between our secrets and our selves. This is also, traditionally, a religious belief, and the connection between religion and secrets is a powerful and enduring one. The Catholic rite of confession is an exemplary model of this. To the priest, I reveal my secrets, and therefore my true self, the self only God otherwise has access to. If I keep secrets from the confessional, the self I offer up to God and the self that God knows to be true are misaligned. I betray God because I withhold information from His representative/proxy on Earth. Thus, Catholic guilt – the guilt of non-confession, the guilt of the unmentioned transgressions in both thought and act. For Derrida, as the philosopher Agata Bielik-Robson points out in her excellent book Derrida’s Marrano Passover (2022), it was his interest in one particular religious group that opened up his own dialogue with the idea of secrets – a group so secret that it forgot that it disguised its own existence even to itself. text:
In the grand tapestry of our lives, there's a thread that often goes unnoticed yet plays an intricate role in shaping who we are and how we make decisions - sleep and dreams. It's almost as if these nocturnal activities exist on the fringes of our consciousness, mere afterthoughts to the daylight hours where we ostensibly 'live'.

But what if I told you that sleep and dreams might not be so inconsequential in our decision-making process? It's a thought that has been quietly bubbling beneath the surface of scientific discourse for quite some time now.

Take, for instance, the well-documented phenomenon of 'sleep on it'. We've all heard the advice - when faced with a tough decision, take some time to sleep on it before making up your mind. There's a reason this advice persists; research suggests that sleeping on a problem can enhance our cognitive abilities and aid in better decision-making.

During REM (rapid eye movement) sleep, the brain is particularly active, showing increased electrical activity similar to wakefulness. This heightened neural activity seems to stimulate the amyggdala, a region associated with emotions, fear responses, and motivation. It's not far-fetched to imagine this could influence decision-making processes by providing new perspectives or even solutions that might have eluded us during waking hours.

Now let's dive into dreams themselves. They are enigmatic entities, often perceived as mere fragments of the subconscious mind. However, they may hold more significance than we give them credit for. Dreams can serve as a sort of mental laboratory where we process and consolidate information from the day. Some researchers argue that dreaming might actually be a form of simulated reality, helping us prepare for real-life scenarios by rehearsing potential outcomes in our sleep.

However, understanding exactly how sleep and dreams impact decision-making remains elusive. It's a complex interplay between neurochemistry, psychology, and physiology that's far from fully understood. Yet, it's clear that these nocturnal activities are integral parts of our lives, subtly influencing the way we perceive reality and make choices.

As I continue to delve deeper into this fascinating subject, I find myself continually struck by the intricacies involved. It's like peeling back layers of an onion, each one revealing fresh complexities that challenge our understanding of ourselves. But isn't it fascinating? To think that even during those hours when we seem most detached from the world, our minds are actively working to help us navigate it better?

There's still so much left unexplored in this realm, so many unanswered questions swirling around like dreams themselves. I can't wait to see where future research takes us on this captivating journey into the twilight zone between wakefulness and slumber.

**HONG KONG:**

STUNNING ANNOUNCEMENT: FEDERAL RESERVE UNVEILS LARGEST BANK MERGER IN HISTORY

In a move that has sent shockwaves through the global financial market, the Federal Reserve has announced the merger of two of America's largest banks - Citibank and JPMorgan Chase. The merger, set to take effect on January 1st, 2022, will create the world's largest bank by assets, with a staggering $8 trillion in combined assets.

The news has sent the Dow Jones Industrial Average plummeting over 700 points in early trading today, as investors grapple with the implications of this landmark merger. The combined entity will be too big to fail, experts warn, potentially creating systemic risks for the global financial system.

"This is a game-changer," said financial analyst John Chen, speaking from his office in New York. "The sheer size of this bank will make it virtually impossible to regulate effectively, and it could create unintended consequences for the broader market."

The merger comes as the Federal Reserve seeks to consolidate power within the banking sector, following a tumultuous decade marked by economic downturns, regulatory reforms, and geopolitical tensions. The two banks have been steadfast in their denials of any talks regarding a merger until today's surprise announcement.

Title: A Frosty Surprise at The Chilly Chef 

Let me just say, I'm a grease-loving eater. I don't shy away from a good burger or a hearty plate of wings. So when the hype surrounding The Chilly Chef reached my ears, I admit, I was skeptical. On a freezing cold day, I decided to brave the chill and give it a try.

As I stepped inside, I felt an immediate warmth envelop me. The cozy interior, with its rustic wooden tables and quaint fairy lights, was a welcome respite from the icy blast outside. My skepticism began to thaw, ever so slightly.

The menu, a delightful mix of comfort food and innovative dishes, left me intrigued. I opted for their signature dish - the Chilly Chef's Chili Mac. As I waited, I couldn't help but notice the pristine cleanliness of the eatery. From the spotless tables to the gleaming counter, it was evident that cleanliness was a priority here.

When my order arrived, I was taken aback by its presentation. The bowl was heaping with a tangled mess of pasta and chili, topped with a generous sprinkle of cheese. But it was the temperature that truly surprised me - it wasn't scalding hot, as I expected, but pleasantly warm, making each bite comfortable despite the frozen fingers outside.

The first spoonful was an unexpected delight. The pasta was perfectly cooked, al dente, and the chili had just the right kick to it. Each mouthful was a symphony of flavors that danced on my taste buds. I found myself questioning my initial skepticism. Was this grease-loving eater's paradise hiding in plain sight?

However, a minor contradiction arose as I delved deeper into the dish. While the chili was delicious, it seemed to lack the 'greasy goodness' that I usually crave. It was leaner than expected for a dish named Chili Mac. This slight discrepancy left me feeling slightly torn - part of me reveled in the flavorful dish before me, while another longed for the comforting grease that I am accustomed to.

In conclusion, The Chilly Chef has certainly won a place in my heart, if not my stomach quite yet. It offers a unique culinary experience that challenges my conventional tastes and provides a warm, inviting atmosphere on even the frostiest of days. Would I return? Absolutely! But next time, maybe I'll try something with a bit more... grease.

Rating:  (3 stars) - A pleasant surprise, but still room for improvement. Worth a visit, but perhaps not my go-to spot just yet.

text: the same time, we should be wary of using humanity to positively equate or compare Latinx kids in cages with their white, middle-class American age-equivalents. These caged kids are not also human; they are extraordinary beings, superhumans, having made incredible, dangerous journeys across lands to escape from the ugly margins of capitalism and empire that made them who they are (and killed many of their peers). Whatever commonalities might exist, unequal structural forces have shaped them into radically different and incommensurable forms of existence. They should be respected and recognised, rather than flattened by providing the deceptive material trappings of a basic humanity. Just as a bar of soap or a flu shot does not give them justice, neither does asserting their essential sameness to rich age-mates growing up in the heart of global empire. Humanity’s abstract universality aims to help us connect to people in very different circumstances, but at the expense of encouraging us to wrongly think of ourselves as like them. At the same time, humanity claims to reach for the good of universal justice, when in reality its claims are shaped by particular Western ideas about justice that have historically oppressed rather than emancipated non-Western others. It might be time to give up on humanity as a byword for emancipation or liberation, and instead call more precisely on what we often ask for in the name of humanity: justice and recognition for those constructed in and deeply marginalised by past and present structures of imperialism, racism, colonialism and capitalism.

The price, which broke the record for a Monet painting at auction, was more than six times what Marsico paid for it in 2002, proving that few investments offer a more buoyant rate of return than a Monet. Star performances by Monet in the auction houses of London and New York have become routine. Yet the exorbitant prices paid for his paintings today don’t stand in any kind of chastening counterpoint to Monet’s commercial fortunes during his own lifetime. The stories of the critical resistance to Impressionism – and consequent financial hardships of the movement’s painters – are, of course, the stuff of popular legend. To be sure, Monet did suffer some early hardships. ‘We have enough of that kind of painting,’ sniffed a juror in 1868, rejecting his works for the annual Paris Salon and thereby scuppering his chances of making sales. That same year, Monet was (no doubt wisely) refused credit for paint, and he supposedly spent one entire winter eating nothing but potatoes. For much of his career, though, he was no starving artist, and the diet of potatoes soon came to be replaced by such favourites as foie gras from Alsace, truffles from Périgord, and lobster in peppercorn sauce. ‘You get the best cuisine in France at his house,’ enthused one visitor treated to the delights of gastronomy chez Monet. The fact is that, after the age of 50 (he lived to be 86), Monet was unrelentingly flush with cash: a happy outcome for such a bon vivant. His wealth kept him in a fleet of automobiles, tweed suits from a fine English tailor in Paris, and a sizeable staff of gardeners to tend his famous gardens. In this respect, he has more in common with Damien Hirst or Jeff Koons, with their high-octane dealers, deep-pocketed collectors, and multimillion-dollar fortunes, than he does with artistic outliers such as the penniless Vincent van Gogh. His surname is an apt one, as headline writers and other punsters appreciate, enjoying its near-homonymity with ‘money’. He was, in fact, the Jazz Age’s answer to Koons, because in 1922, four years before his death, the sale of one of his canvases to a Japanese tycoon made him the world’s most expensive living artist. Monet has long been the go-to artist for the rich and famous. Besides Marsico, recent owners have included David Rockefeller, the Queen Mother, Imelda Marcos, Prince Rainier, the hotelier Steve Wynn, and assorted Russian and Asian billionaires. text:
Dear Reader,

Let's delve into the intricate ethical quagmire that artificial intelligence (AI) has thrust upon us. It's a complex web of questions and dilemmas, a labyrinth that we seem to be navigating blindfolded.

Firstly, consider autonomy. Should AI systems have rights similar to humans? If they're making decisions that affect lives, shouldn't they be accountable? Yet, how can we hold an inanimate object responsible for its actions? The idea is as bizarre as it is compelling.

Next, there's the question of privacy. AI systems need data to learn and function effectively. But this data is often personal, potentially sensitive information. Who gets to decide what data an AI system can access, and how much control do we really have over our digital selves?

Then there's the issue of bias. AI systems are only as good as the data they're trained on. If that data is biased, the AI system will be too. This could lead to discriminatory decisions, further exacerbating societal inequalities. How do we ensure that our AI systems are fair and impartial?

Finally, let's talk about the ethical implications of AI in warfare. Autonomous weapons systems could potentially make life-or-death decisions without human intervention. Should we trust an algorithm with such power? And if a mistake is made, who should be held accountable - the programmer, the military officer, or the AI itself?

These are just a few of the ethical quandaries that AI poses. As we continue to develop and integrate AI into our lives, these questions become more pressing. It's not about whether AI will raise ethical issues, but rather how we choose to address them.

So, dear reader, where do we go from here? How do we ensure that AI serves humanity rather than becoming a threat to it? These are questions we must answer together, as a global community. The future of AI is in our hands, and the choices we make now will shape that future. Let's tread carefully, thoughtfully, and ethically.

Until next time, let these thoughts simmer in your mind. We have much to discuss.

text:
Dear Reader,

Have you ever found yourself grappling with a decision, one that seems to have no clear answer? The kind that keeps you up at night, tossing and turning, mind whirring with possibilities. In moments like these, we often turn to abstract theories, those intangible ideas that we've picked up along our journey through life. They serve as our compass, guiding us when the path ahead is unclear.

I remember a time, not so long ago, when I was faced with such a decision. Should I stay in my comfortable, familiar job, or take a leap of faith and chase after a dream? The choice seemed impossible, yet a small voice inside me whispered, "Chase your passion." It was an abstract theory, one that I'd gleaned from books and mentors, but it was the right path for me.

Abstract theories are not merely academic constructs, reserved for the ivory towers of academia. They are tools we wield in our everyday lives, shaping our thoughts, decisions, and actions. They force us to question our values, to reconsider what truly matters to us. In doing so, they help us carve our own paths, unique to ourselves.

Yet, as powerful as abstract theories can be, they are not without their challenges. They represent ideals, concepts that exist beyond the tangible world we inhabit. And in a world that often values pragmatism and certainty, these ideas can feel elusive, even unattainable.

This tension between the concrete and the abstract is perhaps best encapsulated in our struggle between freedom and belonging. We yearn for autonomy, to carve our own paths, but we also crave connection, a sense of belonging within a larger community. This dance between the two is a complex one, and it's within this tension that abstract theories can help us navigate.

Consider Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs, for example. It posits that we must fulfill our basic needs before we can seek out self-actualization, the pinnacle of human achievement. This theory offers a roadmap for finding balance between freedom and belonging, suggesting that we first need to meet our fundamental needs before we can pursue personal growth.

As I write this, I am struck by how these abstract theories have shaped my life, offering me guidance when I felt lost. They've helped me wrestle with complex questions about what truly matters, pushing me to reconsider my values and strive for more. Yet, they've also reminded me that the path to self-actualization is a long and winding one, filled with challenges and uncertainties.

So, dear reader, I leave you with this thought: The next time you find yourself at a crossroads, remember the abstract theories that have guided you thus far. Embrace their wisdom, let them challenge your values, and help you navigate the complex dance between freedom and belonging. And above all, trust in the journey, for it is through this dance that we find our truest selves.

Yours sincerely,
A Fellow Traveler on Life's Journey

text: René Descartes’s dog, Monsieur Grat (‘Mister Scratch’), used to accompany the 17th-century French philosopher on his ruminative walks, and was the object of his fond attention. Yet, for the most part, Descartes did not think very highly of the inner life of nonhuman animals. ‘[T]he reason why animals do not speak as we do is not that they lack the organs but that they have no thoughts,’ Descartes wrote in a letter in 1646. Followers of Descartes have argued that consciousness is a uniquely human attribute, perhaps facilitated by language, that allows us to communicate and coordinate our memories, sensations and plans over time. On this view, versions of which persist in some quarters today, nonhuman animals are little more than clever automata with a toolkit of preprogrammed behaviours that respond to specific triggers. Insects such as bees and ants are often held up as the epitome of the robotically mechanistic approach to animal nature. Scientists have long known that these creatures must possess a large behavioural repertoire in order to construct their elaborate homes, defend against intruders, and provision their young with food. Yet many still find it plausible to look at bees and ants as little more than ‘reflex machines’, lacking an internal representation of the world, or an ability to foresee even the immediate future. In the absence of external stimuli or internal triggers such as hunger, it’s believed that the insect’s mind is dark and its brain is switched off. Insects are close to ‘philosophical zombies’: hypothetical beings that rely entirely on routines and reflexes, without any awareness. But perhaps the problem is not that insects lack an inner life, but that they don’t have a way to communicate it in terms we can understand. It is hard for us to prise open a window into their minds. So maybe we misdiagnose animal brains as having machine-like properties simply because we understand how machines work – whereas, to date, we have only a fragmentary and imperfect insight into how even the simplest brains process, store and retrieve information. text:
In the heart of a shattered city, under the weight of rubble and despair, I found an unexpected bloom - a fragile sapling pushing through the cracks, its leaves trembling with hope. This sapling was Justice, reemerging from the ashes of conflict. Yet, nestled within its roots, I felt a strange unease, a contradiction that gnawed at my soul: for this tree was also laden with the heavy burden of Forgiveness.

The sun set, casting long shadows over the broken streets, and I found myself pondering these concepts, wrestling with their complexities as I stared at the sapling. Justice, we believe, is clear-cut, righteous, and unyielding. Yet, in the crucible of conflict, it often becomes a weapon wielded by one side against another, a tool used to justify retribution rather than peace.

Forgiveness, on the other hand, feels like a delicate feather caught in a storm - easily shattered, yet somehow resilient. It is the antidote to hate, the balm that heals wounds. But how do we forgive when the pain runs so deep, when the scars are still raw and tender? How can we find it within ourselves to let go of anger and resentment, to extend a hand in reconciliation?

As I walked through the ghostly streets, the weight of these questions heavy upon my shoulders, I realized that our understanding of Justice and Forgiveness is often limited, distorted by our own biases, fears, and pain. We cling to notions of retribution and revenge, seeking vengeance for past wrongs rather than healing and redemption.

In post-conflict societies, we see the consequences of this misunderstanding - cycles of violence that seem endless, wounds that refuse to heal, and a collective inability to move forward. We become trapped in a web of our own making, bound by chains forged from anger, resentment, and a refusal to forgive.

Yet, amidst the chaos, there are glimmers of hope - moments when we catch a glimpse of the possibility of forgiveness, the potential for Justice to transform into something greater. We see individuals reaching out across divides, offering olive branches in place of swords, and extending their hands in friendship instead of enmity.

But the road to forgiveness is fraught with peril - it is a journey that requires courage, resilience, and an unwavering belief in the power of redemption. It demands that we confront our own pain, acknowledge the wrongs that have been done, and find within ourselves the capacity to forgive.

As I stood before the sapling, the leaves trembling in the breeze, I couldn't help but feel a sense of hope - for I knew that within each of us lies the potential for greatness, the ability to transcend our own pain and seek forgiveness. But I also felt a deep sadness, a recognition that this journey is not an easy one, that it requires a strength and wisdom beyond my own understanding.

And so, as I walked away from the sapling, I was left with more questions than answers - questions about the nature of Justice and Forgiveness, about our capacity to heal and redeem, and about the limits of human understanding. But I also felt a spark within me, a determination to continue exploring these ideas, to seek out the wisdom that lies at their heart.

In the end, perhaps the most important thing we can do is to remember the sapling - to hold fast to the belief that Justice and Forgiveness are not separate entities, but intertwined branches growing from the same roots. And maybe, just maybe, if we can learn to nurture both, we can help our world heal, one step at a time.

Title: A So-So Experience at Mane Manor Hair Salon 

I stumbled into Mane Manor Hair Salon one busy Saturday afternoon, seeking a quick trim before a friend's wedding. The salon was packed to the brim with chatty patrons and stylists bustling about, giving off a lively energy that made me feel like I had walked into a buzzing beehive.

Upon being seated, I was greeted by my stylist, Rachel. She was friendly and professional, which instantly put me at ease despite the chaos around us. We chatted about our shared love for Korean drama series while she deftly snipped away at my locks. Her expertise was evident in her ability to quickly gauge my desired length and style without any fuss or confusion.

The salon's cleanliness was a bit of a mixed bag. On one hand, the styling stations were spotless, each equipped with brand new tools and sparkling combs. However, the bathroom could have been a tad more sanitized. I found myself wishing for some air freshener or hand soap, but alas, it was not to be.

Now, here comes the contradiction: Despite the initial chaos, I must admit that the salon's atmosphere was actually quite soothing once I settled into my chair. The soft hum of hairdryers and chatter filled the air like a comforting white noise, and the gentle scents of various hair products combined to create an oddly calming aroma.

When it came time to pay, I felt compelled to tip generously due to Rachel's exceptional service. However, as someone who values excellent customer service, I couldn't help but feel slightly disappointed that my total bill was on the higher end for what seemed like a standard haircut. It's always a bit tricky trying to balance appreciation for great service with budget constraints, isn't it?

Overall, Mane Manor Hair Salon is certainly not a bad place to get your hair done. Rachel was skilled and attentive, the atmosphere was oddly relaxing (once I got past the initial chaos), and the salon was generally clean, save for the bathroom. If you're in the area and need a quick trim, I would recommend giving them a try  just don't forget to bring your own air freshener for the bathroom!

Title: Cozy Korean BBQ Haven on a Frosty Day 

Had the pleasure of visiting Seoul Grill House on a bone-chilling winter day, and let me tell you, it was nothing short of a delightful escape from the cold! I'm not one to enjoy waiting in line (especially when it's freezing outside), but their efficient service managed to keep the wait time minimal.

Upon entering, I was immediately greeted by the warm, inviting aroma of sizzling meats and savory marinades that seemed to embrace you like a comforting hug on a cold day. The restaurant's cozy ambiance, with soft lighting and traditional Korean decor, made it feel as though we had been transported straight to Seoul without even leaving the city!

The service was swift and friendly, and our server graciously guided us through the menu, offering recommendations on the best dishes to try. We opted for a variety of meats, along with some mouthwatering side dishes (banchan) that included pickled radish, spicy cabbage, and crispy seaweed salad.

The star of the show, however, was undoubtedly the meat! Each piece was expertly marinated and cooked to perfection, leaving us savoring every bite. The beef bulgogi, in particular, stood out for its tender texture and well-balanced flavors. And let's not forget the garlic rice  a must-try side that perfectly complemented our savory feast!

Now, I know this might sound like an advertorial, but trust me when I say that Seoul Grill House truly is a hidden gem worth visiting. If you find yourself craving some comforting Korean BBQ on a frigid day (or any other day, for that matter), this spot should be at the top of your list! Just remember to bundle up before waiting in line  but trust me, it'll be worth it once you step inside and take a bite of their delectable dishes. 

Overall, I highly recommend Seoul Grill House for a cozy Korean BBQ experience that warms your heart (and belly) on even the frostiest days!

text: Singapore: Oil prices edged up in Asia Thursday following signs of a slight improvement in US demand but concerns that an oversupply will persist past next year kept the commodity struggling at multi priced oil cheaper. But analysts also noted that the unit will likely to gather strength before a Fed meeting next week in which the US central bank is widely expected to raise interest rates. US benchmark West Texas Intermediate for delivery in January was up 23 cents at $37.39 and Brent crude for January was trading 33 cents higher at $40.44 at around 0300 GMT. Prices have slumped about nine percent since Fridays decision by the OPEC oil exporters club not to cut output despite pumping more than its target in the face of a global oversupply and weakness in the world economy. There was some good news when the Department of Energy said US Wednesday oil inventories fell 3.6 million barrels in the week ending December 4, a booster for prices as it indicates an uptick in demand. However, Sanjeev Gupta, who heads the Asia Pacific oil and gas practice at professional services firm EY, said: "The market remains weak overall due to global oversupply, compounded by the OPECs decision to leave its production quota unchanged."Investors will closely monitor the Federal Reserves policy dominated commodities will become more expensive if US dollar appreciates," he said. In a market commentary on Thursday, BMI Research said it expects prices to remain range-bound as the oil glut "persists to 2018".

text: ISLAMABAD: Finance Minister, Ishaq Dar on Monday said the government is ready to set up a parliamentary committee over the ordinance issued a day earlier for conversion of Pakistan International Airlines (PIA) into a limited company. Issuing a policy statement from the floor of National Assembly, the Finance Minister said prior to reaching any conclusion the opposition should first go through the ordinance. Defending the issuance of the ordinance, he said the government adhered to all the legal requirements in promulgating this ordinance.We are restructuring PIA and the purpose of the ordinance is to make it a corporate entity, he argued, adding that implementation of the ordinance will not lead to any kind of change in the rights and privileges of the PIA employees. He said if the opposition has objections over the ordinance, it can go ahead and present a resolution for its rejection in the lower house. He said the matter will be discussed with the opposition leader in the National Assembly and thereupon the speaker may constitute a committee to look into the issue. Defending the governments decision to impose additional taxes, Ishaq Dar said there was a tax shortfall of Rs40 billion. He said taxes have either been imposed or raised only items that fall in the category of luxury goods. He said 58 percent of the proceeds from additional taxes will go to the provinces kitty while the federation will only receive its one third.

Another regulation aimed at curbing excessive risk-taking within banks is causing stirs among Goldman Sachs' high-net-worth clients. Known as the "Risk Management Overhaul," this rule limits the types of investments that banks can make and imposes stricter controls on their trading activities.

"I've made a lot of money with Goldman Sachs over the years, but I understand why they have to tighten things up," admitted 62-year-old investor Michael Patel from London. "It's just disappointing that some of the more lucrative opportunities might disappear as a result."

text: Ten years ago, one of the most disruptive events in my intellectual life occurred at a dinner party at my house. My friend Richard Thomas, who had just given a talk at Baylor University, mentioned that a student of his had discovered an ‘Isaiah acrostic’ in Vergil’s Georgics, a 1st-century BCE poem ostensibly about farming but really about life and the universe. This remark simultaneously opened the door to two phenomena in ancient Greek and Latin poetry that I had not really thought about, despite a lifelong career in Classics: acrostics and Judaism. The relationship between the biblical and the classical traditions has always been fraught. As Tertullian testily asked in his screed against pagan writers: ‘What has Athens to do with Jerusalem?’ Similarly, St Jerome felt compelled to abandon the classical authors he loved after a nightmare vision in which the judge accused him of being a Ciceronian, not a Christian. One of the many reasons Vergil is central to the Western tradition is that his Fourth Eclogue, which portrays the birth of a miraculous boy ‘sent down from heaven’ to inaugurate a new Golden Age, helped calm these fears: it was seen by readers from late antiquity until the 18th century as a pagan prophecy of the birth of Christ, thereby allowing Christianity to assimilate the Classics rather than merely rejecting them. Post-Enlightenment readers, however, tended to react against the Christian interpretation – they had no desire to view Athens through the prism of Jerusalem. Whatever one may think about the supernatural dimension, there is abundant evidence for personal and intellectual contact between Jews and non-Jewish Greeks and Romans before and after the birth of Christ. Jews composed something like 10-20 per cent of the population of the Roman Empire; there are many overt references to Jews and Judaism in classical texts; and the Septuagint – the Greek translation of the Hebrew scriptures undertaken in the 3rd century BCE – would have been accessible to educated non-Jewish people throughout the Mediterranean world. Classicists and intellectual historians should be paying far more attention than they presently do to the impact of Jewish texts and culture on classical authors. In my paper ‘Was Vergil Reading the Bible?’ (2018), I argued that the answer to that question is probably ‘Yes’, and that at least some scholars are beginning to realise that Jewish themes are an important component of his meaning. Scholars’ lack of attention to acrostics, on the other hand, may stem more from an intuitive sense that they are beneath the dignity of sophisticated authors. Indeed, acrostics are an art form simple enough for a child to create. The answer depends on what happens after we die. Most religions hold that our consciousness and identity persist after death, in one otherworldly realm or another, but it’s rarely the same one. Indeed, there are a wide variety of possible destinations for those who believe in an afterlife, most of them assigned according to our behaviour during life. Some of these places are better than life as we know it, but some are worse. If there is an afterlife, whether you want to live a long life or a short one would depend to some extent on whether your afterlife would be an upgrade or a demotion. Those who believe that they are going to hell, or even somewhere just slightly less agreeable than Earth, would be wise to eat right, exercise and look both ways before crossing the street. To those bound for a better place, on the other hand, an early demise has something to recommend it. There are a few living authors who claim to have visited heaven, but judging from their cheerful media appearances, that first hit of eternal bliss hasn’t left them overly desperate for another. Heaven is no heroin, apparently. The story of Colton Burpo, the four-year-old boy whose trip to the great beyond inspired his father’s book Heaven is for Real (2010), reassures us that our souls resemble ourselves in our 20s and 30s, thereby undercutting another reason to long for a premature end. There’s no need to die young in order to look your best for the pearly gates, if strolling in as your ideal self is part of the deal. In any case, the longest conceivable human life is so minute compared with the unfathomable enormity of forever that, if there is an eternal afterlife, any mortal span will vanish into statistical insignificance by comparison. Title: A Delightful Ice Cream Haven with Unmatched Service 

Hey Yelpers!

I recently visited this amazing ice cream parlor, and let me tell you, it was a truly unforgettable experience. The wait time was minimal, despite the crowd, which is always a plus for me since I'm never one to linger in lines.

The moment I stepped inside, I was greeted by the intoxicating aroma of freshly-churned ice cream that immediately transported me back to my childhood summers. The vibrant, welcoming ambiance added an extra layer of charm to this already enchanting establishment.

Now, let's talk about the service. Oh boy, was it exceptional! The staff were attentive, friendly, and genuinely seemed to care that I had a fantastic experience. They took their time to explain each flavor in detail, even suggesting a couple based on my preference for nutty flavors (I'm a total sucker for pistachio).

I decided to go with the 'Cosmic Swirl,' a delightful blend of creamy vanilla and swirling ribbons of dark chocolate that tasted like a dream. I also picked up a couple of scoops of their signature 'Peanut Butter Perfection' for my friends who were joining me later.

The only minor contradiction in my experience was the unexpectedly heavy container they gave me to carry home. Granted, it was overflowing with delicious ice cream, but I couldn't help but wish they had offered a more lightweight option as an eco-friendly alternative. Still, it's a small price to pay for such delectable treats!

Chinese investors were considering investing in infrastructural development, leather, readymade garments, pharmaceuticals, automobile and other sectors. Bangladesh has already agreed to set up an industrial park exclusively for Chinese investors near the strategic port of Chittagong hoping it will attract manufacturers seeking cheaper labour than they can find at home. As a mark of their deepening economic ties, the two nations on Friday also signed an agreement to conduct a feasibility study on a China free and quota-free access for its products in the Chinese market, the worlds second largest economy. Trade between the two nations is currently heavily skewed in Chinas favour with Bangladesh exporting goods worth only about $808 million last year while its imports from China stood at $9.64 billion. Xi hailed what he called a "historical turning point" in relations with Bangladesh after talks with Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina. text:
In the quiet confines of an old, musty library, I found myself lost in the pages of a forgotten tome. The book spoke of justice in unequal societies, a topic that has haunted my thoughts for years. As I traced the faded ink with my fingers, I couldn't help but be reminded of a conversation I had years ago with an old friend, a man who hailed from a land where inequality was the norm.

He told me about his daily struggle, about the unjust laws that made his life a constant battle. He spoke of a system that was designed to keep certain people down while others thrived. His words echoed in my mind as I read the book, and I found myself contemplating the paradox of justice in unequal societies.

Justice, as we understand it, is the concept of fairness and impartiality. It is a beacon that guides us towards righteousness, a shield that protects the weak from the strong. But what happens when this beacon is dimmed by inequality? When the scales are tipped in favor of some while others are left to languish in poverty and despair?

In such societies, justice becomes a luxury, a privilege reserved for the few. The laws that should protect all citizens become weapons wielded by the powerful against the powerless. The strong use their influence to bend the rules to their advantage, leaving the weak with no recourse but to endure their suffering in silence.

But is this truly justice? Can a system that allows for such blatant inequality be just? Can a society that condemns its most vulnerable citizens to a life of hardship be considered fair? I argue that it cannot. Justice, by its very nature, demands equality. It demands that every individual, regardless of their station in life, be treated with dignity and respect.

Yet, in unequal societies, justice is often a mirage. A tantalizing promise dangled just out of reach for those who need it most. The pursuit of justice becomes a Sisyphean task, as the oppressed are left to labor under the weight of an unjust system.

And so, we must ask ourselves: How do we bring justice to unequal societies? How do we level the playing field and ensure that every individual has access to the protection they deserve?

There are no easy answers to these questions. The path to justice in such societies is fraught with obstacles and pitfalls. But I believe that it begins with a willingness to acknowledge the injustices that exist and a commitment to work towards change. It requires a concerted effort by individuals, communities, and governments to dismantle the systems that perpetuate inequality and build a new foundation upon which justice can truly flourish.

As I continued to read, my mind wandered to the countless other societies around the world that grapple with this same struggle. To those who are suffering under an unjust system, I offer you my solidarity. Together, we must continue to fight for a future where justice is not a privilege but a right, a future where every individual can live free from the chains of inequality.

But as I closed the book and stepped out into the cold night air, I knew that this was just the beginning of the discussion. The path ahead is long and winding, filled with challenges and setbacks. But I am hopeful, for I know that in the pursuit of justice, there is no turning back.

As I walked home under the stars, I couldn't help but think about my friend and the life he leads. I pray that one day, he will be able to live free from the shackles of inequality, free to pursue a life of dignity and respect. And as I drifted off to sleep, I made a silent promise to myself: I will not rest until justice is truly realized in every corner of our world.

text:
In the quiet corners of my mind, where thoughts often gather like leaves on a windless day, I find myself pondering an intriguing paradox: the role of abstract theory in our everyday decision-making. It's a labyrinthine maze that seems to challenge traditional assumptions about how we navigate life.

On one hand, we are beings of concrete action and immediate response. We make decisions based on what we see, feel, hear, and touch. The world around us is tangible, visceral, and demands an instant reaction. Yet, on the other hand, we are also creatures of thought, capable of pondering the unseen, the hypothetical, the abstract. We create theories, models, and systems to help us understand this vast, complex universe.

The question that lingers is: How do these two realms intersect? How does our everyday decision-making incorporate these abstract theories we so meticulously craft?

Consider a simple example: buying a car. We might initially make decisions based on what feels good, what looks attractive, or what fits our immediate needs. But as we delve deeper into the process, we start to consider fuel efficiency, safety ratings, and reliability - abstract concepts that require some degree of understanding and interpretation. We're not just buying a car; we're making an investment, a commitment, a theory about the future.

This intersection is not always smooth, though. Theories can be complex, difficult to grasp, and prone to error. They often require a certain level of expertise and education, which not everyone has access to. This creates a disparity in our decision-making abilities, a gap between those who can navigate the abstract and those who cannot.

Moreover, these theories are not always accurate or applicable. They are products of their time, influenced by societal norms, cultural biases, and individual perspectives. They can limit our understanding rather than expand it, if we blindly follow them without questioning or challenging their assumptions.

Yet, despite these challenges, abstract theory plays a crucial role in our decision-making. It allows us to predict, to plan, to strategize. It helps us make sense of the world, even when things are chaotic and uncertain. It's not just about numbers and formulas; it's about understanding patterns, relationships, and connections.

As I sit here, lost in thought, I can't help but wonder: How do we strike a balance between the tangible and the abstract? How do we ensure that our theories are accessible, accurate, and beneficial to all? These questions, I fear, may never have a definitive answer. But they are worth pondering, worth exploring, worth challenging our assumptions about decision-making and the role of abstract theory in it.

It would be a relief to heal the rift between traditional Darwinian thinking and believers in the possibility of Gaia Songs don’t themselves reproduce, but they are re-produced and do evolve. The current nitrogen cycle is not that of the Archaean Earth, but it can be seen as its continuation, insofar as earlier cycles stimulated the evolution of species that then evolved to perform later versions. Indeed, the nitrogen cycle could be viewed as the equivalent of the surname Jones, in my earlier parable. If songs are cast as persisters (in place of replicators/reproducers) and the various species that periodically assemble to implement them are cast as their interactors, Hull’s replicator/interactor framework has considerable explanatory power. A problem here might lie in the implication that processes or patterns of interaction, which are arguably not material things, can cause the evolution of species, which are. It’s not clear that the Jones family name itself has an impact on the reproductive behaviour of Joneses, though confidence-building family legends about reproductive prowess might do. But this dilemma is not unique to ITSNTS, in any case. In fact, Dawkins himself considered that genes as information are eternal, while their material instantiations, physical genes and the vehicles they produce are ephemeral – the ‘lumbering robots’ recruited to serve their selfish ends. Of this view, the evolutionary biologist David Haig writes: [M]aterial genes were physical objects but informational genes were the abstract sequences of which material genes were temporary vehicles. Material genes were identified with gene tokens and informational genes with gene types … Continuity resides in the recursive representation of immortal pattern by ephemeral avatars …So the immateriality/materiality problem is not a new one. Moreover, it at least seems to be lessened by adopting a replicator/interactor framework, where ‘perpetuation’ can mean differential persistence – persistence doesn’t require continuous presence – replication is taken to include reproduction, recurrence and re-production – and processes such as the nitrogen cycle can be considered entities that are perpetuated (the replicators, as it were). Beyond the benefit to science, ‘Darwinising Gaia’ would also have some political benefits. It might encourage us to look at nature as a coherent whole, with an evolutionary trajectory that we can foster or deflect as we choose. After all, we are already doing that, whether we realise it or not. Certainly, it would be a relief to heal the rift between traditional Darwinian thinking and believers in the possibility of Gaia, though there’s still much work to do to cement and validate the theory. And we’ll never really know what Darwin might have accepted as ‘Darwinian’, had he lived another 138 years. I’m just hoping that he’d applaud these efforts to render Gaia acceptable within a selectionist framework, and that he wouldn’t think we’d stretched his grand theory past the breaking point.

text: It is a familiar story. A superpower goes to war and faces a stronger-than-expected insurgency in distant lands, yet has insufficient forces to counter it because of political and military constraints. The superpower decides to hire contractors, some of whom are armed, to support its war effort. The armed contractors prove to be both a blessing and a curse, providing vital security services to the campaign, yet at times killing innocent civilians, causing strategic setbacks, and damaging the superpower’s legitimacy. Without these contractors, the superpower could not wage the war. With them, it is more difficult to win. The armed contractors in question are not in Iraq or Afghanistan but in northern Italy, and the year is not 2007 but 1377. The superpower is not the United States but the papacy under Pope Gregory XI, fighting the antipapal league led by the duchy of Milan. The tragic killing of civilians by armed contractors did not occur in Baghdad but in Cesena, 630 years earlier. The military companies employed were not DynCorp International, Triple Canopy or Blackwater, but the Company of the Star, the Company of the Hat and the White Company. Known as free companies, these for-profit warriors were organised as corporations, with a well-articulated hierarchy of subcommanders and administrative machinery that oversaw the fair distribution of loot according to employees’ contracts. CEO-like captains led these medieval military corporations. The parallels between medieval and contemporary private military companies (PMCs) are strong. Today, the US and many others hire contractors to fulfil security-related contracts in the world’s most dangerous places. In the late Middle Ages, such men were called condottieri – literally, ‘contractors’ – who agreed to perform security services described in written contracts, or condotte. Both modern and medieval contractors were organised as companies, their services available to the highest or most powerful bidder for profit. Both filled their ranks with professional men of arms drawn from different countries and loyal primarily to the paycheck. Both have functioned as private armies, usually offering land-based combat skills rather than naval (or aerial) capabilities and deploying force in a military manner rather than as law enforcement or police. Mercenaries are back. Once brandished as villainous outlaws, they are emerging from the shadows to once again become a mainstream instrument of world politics. The United Arab Emirates (UAE) has hired hundreds of Latin-American mercenaries to fight the Iranian-backed Houthis in Yemen. After years of struggling against Boko Haram, Nigeria finally employed mercenaries to do the job, and they did. text: Brea really does collect a huge diversity of the biota from the Late Pleistocene,’ Harris told me. ‘We have well over 600 species of animals and plants from the last phase of the last Ice Age, and there’s greater diversity preserved here than any other deposit of similar age.’ And the sample size of many of those species is staggering. ‘If people want to study living wolves,’ Harris said, ‘they’re lucky if a museum has 20. Here, we have more than 4,000 dire wolves.’ As the camel picks, chews, swallows and starts to digest the shrubby plants, isotopes of carbon and nitrogen from those plants become assimilated into its tissues and bones Unlike the charismatic megamammals, many of the 600 species pulled from La Brea are still around. ‘Most animals smaller than a coyote are still living today,’ Harris explained, ‘but they’re not in this neck of the woods.’ For example, a particular species of snail found in the asphalt is today only found in cooler, wetter habitats at elevations about 5,000ft in California and Arizona. This incredibly detailed understanding of the plants and animals of the time is made possible by La Brea’s natural asphalt. ‘Asphalt acts as a wonderful preservative,’ Harris says. In most cases, bones trapped in asphalt are not even mineralised, and are in fact the original bone of the actual animals. They still count as fossils, but they are not like stone dinosaur bones. When a museum volunteer uncovers a Smilodon skull, they are touching the actual, stained bone of the big cat. Early 20th century paleontologists boiled the bones in kerosene to clean off the tar and generally paid little attention to the microfossils that littered the sludge that came off the skeletal parts. Today, researchers at La Brea take great care to get every fossil they can. Not only are the bones so carefully collected and documented that the entire bonebed can be reconstruct from notes, but the matrix surrounding those bones is also labelled so that its association with a particular grid square is recorded. That matrix is then treated with biodiesel to reduce many pounds of sediment and asphalt into siftable slurry that yields small fossils. These are some of the most important clues in the collection. Sabercats and mammoths attract the most visitor attention but the small fossils fill out the ancient environment with a much greater degree of resolution. And even when bones go into the lab, volunteers spend a considerable amount of time picking out those little fossils that have been preserved inside skull cavities or in the matrix clinging to bones. As my friend and Page Museum volunteer Herb Schiff told me while showing off a dire wolf skull: ‘They chain you to your chair until you identify all the little bones.’ A pain in the ass for volunteers, perhaps, but a bonanza for researchers. They cut through the thin, cold air in supersonic aeroshells and parachutes, making planetfall in bouncing airbag cocoons and bursts of braking retrorockets, bristling with cameras and spectrometers, antennas and manipulator arms. Sniffing the air, sifting through soil, wheeling across a cratered landscape, the robots doggedly seek any wisp of Martian moisture, like lost desert travellers dying of thirst. They are looking for life, following a strategy that NASA officials describe as ‘following the water’. This strategy pays heed to water’s eerie harmony with life: wherever one is found on Earth, the other isn’t far behind. There are good reasons to believe that water is the ideal cornerstone of biology, not only on Earth, but elsewhere in the Universe. All you need to make it is hydrogen and oxygen, respectively the first and third most abundant elements in the cosmos. In liquid form, water is unparalleled in its attunement to life’s needs. It facilitates the transmission of biochemical energy, nutrients, and waste. It shapes the structures and interactions of proteins and other macromolecules. It shields against hostile cosmic radiation, and it has a remarkably robust capacity for retaining warmth. Most strangely, unlike almost every other substance in the Universe, when water freezes, it expands instead of contracting, forming a protective, insulating shell of ice at its surface. This helps oceans, lakes, and other liquid reservoirs avoid freezing solid when exposed to prolonged cold. For researchers who study Mars, following the water has paid off handsomely. Every mission sent to Mars seeking water has found it and, as a result, we now know that our neighbouring world used to be a warmer, wetter, more habitable place. Officially, the next stage of NASA’s Mars exploration programme is to ‘seek signs of life’, a phase that supposedly began in the summer of 2012 with the successful landing of the car-sized Curiosity rover. It’s true that Curiosity can sniff out organic carbon in samples of Martian rock and soil, but it mostly seems to be following the water, too. So far, it has rolled through what looks to be an ancient dried-up riverbed, and found evidence suggesting that some of Mars’s ancient water was fresh enough to drink. Lawrence is too turbulent, too meteoric for that. Instead, Lawrence was both stimulus and provocation. There was an emancipatory quality in reading him, because he never thought about what was expected of him, but examined his own thoughts by a bright, exacting light and took seriously what it might mean to think the opposite. He thought we’d become too self-conscious, and that we inflicted self-consciousness on our children by loving them too much. text: Tried Bombay Spice last night. Success on both counts! It was Saturday at about 6:30 and we were seated immediately. The place was almost full. We sat on the patio, which was lovely, but might get too hot during the summer (I didn't see any misters--You're Welcome, Mother Earth!). Service was attentive, and our waitress gave us several recommendations. We ordered: Somosas (baked, I think, not fried, which made them a little messier to eat but still very very tasty). Naan (chewy, moist, naan-y...yum) Grilled Shrimp skewers (a little on the skimpy side, but very tasty, and served with grilled peppers and onions and a delightful mint sauce). We each ordered individual entrees with basmati. They encourage you to order the two-entree choice, but we were trying to avoid over-ordering, and it turned out to be exactly the right amount of food. I ordered the Cauliflower and potatoes, which was moist, savory, a little spicy, and very tasty, albeit lacking a little in the sauce department (I would have liked a little something to spoon over my rice). Husband ordered the Chicken Tikka Masala, which was exceptional. They claim that they don't use cream in their food, so I don't know how they made the masala sauce so creamy and good, but they did and I'm not going to question it any more! I would have licked the bowl if we had been at home. Next time I think we'll get some apps again but try the Bombay Plate to share, which is 5 entree choices and rice. That way we can try a whole bunch of things. $35 before tip for naan, two appetizers, and two single-choice entrees. Not bad for a Saturday out. We'll definitely be back, even though it was kind of a drive for us.
