However, even discounting death itself – no mean feat – there is a wide range of appalling fates that can befall any child that is brought into existence: starvation, rape, abuse, assault, serious mental illness, infectious disease, malignancy, paralysis. These cause vast amounts of suffering before the person dies. Prospective parents impose these risks on the children they create. The magnitude of the risk obviously varies, depending on such factors as one’s geographical and temporal locations, and one’s sex. Even controlling for these variables, the lifetime risks are often difficult to quantify. For example, rape is significantly underreported, but there are conflicting data on just how underreported it is. Similarly, studies on mental diseases such as major depressive disorders often underestimate the lifetime risk, in part because some of the subjects have not yet experienced the depression that will later affect them. Even if we take the low estimates, in the cumulative risks of all the different misfortunes that can befall people, the odds are stacked deeply against any child. The risks of cancer alone are substantial: in the United Kingdom, roughly 50 per cent of people will develop the disease. If people imposed that sort of risk of that sort of harm on others in non-procreative contexts, they would be very widely condemned. The same standards should be applied to procreation. The foregoing arguments all criticise procreation on the grounds of what procreation does to the person who is brought into existence. These I call philanthropic arguments for anti-natalism; there is also a misanthropic argument. What is distinctive about this argument is that it criticises procreation on the grounds of the harm that the created person will (likely) do. It is presumptively wrong to create new beings that are likely to cause significant harm to others. Homo sapiens is the most destructive species, and vast amounts of this destruction are wreaked on other humans. Humans have killed one another since the origin of the species, but the scale (not rate) of killing has expanded (not least because there are now so many more humans to kill than there were for most of human history). The means by which many millions of humans have been killed have been dismally diverse. They include stabbing, hacking, slashing, hanging, gassing, poisoning, drowning, and bombing. Humans also visit other horrors on their fellows, including persecuting, oppressing, beating, branding, maiming, tormenting, torturing, raping, kidnapping, and enslaving. The optimists argue that prospective children are unlikely to be among the perpetrators of such evil, and this is true: only a small proportion of children will become perpetrators of the worst barbarities against humans. text: Daniel Kahneman observed that people often take an approach to reasoning that can be described as ‘anchoring and adjustment’. Presented with some piece of information – the asking price for a condo, early results from exit polls, a sample answer to a trivia question – people tend to use that information as a starting point in formulating their own estimates, hypotheses and predictions. If I ask you to guess the lifespan of the average tortoise, after first asking you whether most tortoises live past the age of nine, you’re likely to name a lower number than your friend who was asked whether they often live past the age of 70. The same cognitive logic is what makes red herrings so effective. They exert a gravitational pull on our speculation. Information can also act as an anchor when we know it’s false or even irrelevant. If you recognise some false trail as a red herring, it can still affect what Tversky and Kahneman described as the ‘availability’ of other ideas. It will constrain your imagination and make some lines of reasoning much easier to see than others. Characters make particularly useful vessels for misleading information, because they add deniability. A character can lie or be mistaken without making the story as a whole inconsistent or dishonest. This handy fact works nicely with another predictable feature of human cognition: our relatively bad memory for source information. Especially if we’re distracted (say, by some compelling, emotionally engaging story content), we can encounter claims in dubious settings, or even see them being debunked, but later remember only that we’ve heard them somewhere before. Then if we encounter the claims again, that sense of familiarity can make us more likely to take them seriously. The psychologist Daniel Schacter, a major figure in the cognitive and neurological study of memory, calls this failing and others like it the ‘sins of memory’, and stories can use them to plant information in places where we are likely to remember the misleading bits while losing track of the caveats. Fortunately, despite our tendency to lose track of this information, we never fully lose track of the distinction, so if a story shows us that a source we trusted was unreliable, we get it. The source is blown, but the story itself can still be on the up-and-up. If stories play their cards right, they can even introduce entirely new information at the dénouement but finesse the introduction so that it doesn’t seem to be new. A very common and surprisingly dependable example is the case of the underspecified clue: Sherlock Holmes observes the knees of a suspect’s trousers, but ‘reminds’ Watson of the fact that they were dirty and worn only as the solution to the mystery is revealed. text:
**LOS ANGELES, CA - A Stunning Absence**

In a city where the sun perpetually shines bright, a shadow has fallen. The Los Angeles Lakers, an institution synonymous with basketball greatness, will be absent from the playoffs for the first time in over a decade. This seismic shift ripples far beyond the Staples Center's hallowed courts.

The Purple and Gold's fall from grace is a tale of injuries, age, and an unforgiving sport that leaves no room for error. Injuries to superstars LeBron James and Anthony Davis sidelined them for significant portions of the season, disrupting their rhythm and chemistry. The duo, when healthy, has been a formidable force, but this year they simply couldn't carry the team on their shoulders alone.

LeBron, 36, is human after all, his once unstoppable physique showing signs of wear. He battled an abdominal strain and a high ankle sprain, playing through pain to keep the Lakers in contention. Davis, 28, suffered a serious foot injury early in the season, his return not enough to spark a turnaround.

But the Lakers' struggles extend beyond their injured stars. A roster filled with aging talent failed to deliver consistent performances. Veterans like Montrezl Harrell and Dennis Schrder, who joined the team during the offseason, underperformed relative to expectations. The youngsters, meanwhile, showed promise but lacked the maturity and experience needed to consistently compete at the highest level.

The absence of the Lakers from the playoffs is felt most acutely in the international basketball community. In countries like France, where Davis hails from, and England, LeBron's adopted home, the Lakers have always been a source of pride. Their success has inspired a new generation of players to dream big, to aim for greatness. Now, their absence leaves a void, a question mark hanging over the game they love.

Yet, this is not a story of despair, but of resilience and determination. The Lakers have weathered storms before, and they will do so again. They have the resources, the talent, and perhaps most importantly, the drive to return to the playoffs next season.

In the meantime, the world watches with bated breath. Will the Lakers rebound? Or is this the beginning of a new era in Los Angeles basketball? Only time will tell. But one thing is certain: the game is never over until the final buzzer sounds.

text: Most of the time, getting a marriage licence is an administrative transaction like any other. The piece of paper, the legality of matrimony, is an errand, a bureaucratic checkmark. The real celebration comes later. The everyday realities of marriage arrive later still. But what happens when that order is reversed? When you’ve had the ceremony and the long years together but no piece of paper, because it wasn’t possible, because your society didn’t allow it? What happens when, suddenly, that sanction is overturned? On a cold, rainy night in Seattle last December, I saw such a quiet revolution in action. Several hundred couples — some of them couples who had already spent decades together, who had been ‘domestic partners’ for years, who had long since held grand but not legal weddings before their friends and families — lined up for the part of the matrimonial process that most couples do first: the marriage licence. The result, that night, was a strange mixture of beauty and bureaucracy. Couples kissed and cried as they spent hours in line and filled out paperwork. They carried flowers, tiaras, and signs declaring their years together — as well as lawn chairs, blankets, and thermos flasks. To pass the hours, they shared their stories of meeting each other and falling in love, reflecting with wonder or anger on the time when they thought this day would never come. Some could still hardly believe that, in just a few hours, at 12:01 am, Washington’s law extending the right of marriage to same-sex couples would go into effect. On one side it read, ‘20 years together.’ On the other: ‘Just married’ The law had actually passed the state legislature more than nine months earlier, but opponents tried to block it by demanding it be put to a popular vote. No state in the nation had ever directly voted in favor of gay marriage — in fact, citizens of 30 states have voted to write bans on same-sex marriage into their very constitutions. Washington’s opponents of gay marriage felt confident that the people would strike this law down, too. But Americans are rapidly changing their minds about gay marriage. In the decade prior to Washington’s law, according to the Pew Research Center, public opinion on legalising same-sex marriage moved from a majority in opposition (54 per cent) and a minority in support (35 per cent) to roughly equal numbers for and against. In 2012, support for marriage equality took a clear lead for the first time, with 48 per cent in favour, and 43 per cent against. Which tack to take might depend partly on whether one thinks life sentences are ever a just and appropriate punishment. Proponents of levelling up can’t simply assume the answer is yes, when that is the very question at issue. Here is one reason to think levelling down is appropriate. While the US criminal justice system treats Black and brown people worse than it treats whites, it seems plausible that the treatment of white people adheres more closely to our society’s views of justice, since white people are thought to represent the ‘normal’ or the default. People of colour, then, are subject to a surplus penalty rather than white people being let off easy. If that is so, reducing the penalties Black people pay rather than increasing those of whites would bring us closer to justice. You cannot reasonably believe that someone should spend their whole life in prison unless you believe they will always present a significant threat to public safety, or that they deserve such a sentence. I have already argued that the overwhelming majority of the more than 200,000 people currently serving life sentences in the US do not present a lifelong threat to public safety. Whether they ought to spend their whole lives in prison depends, then, solely on whether they deserve it. They can deserve it only if they are fully responsible for their criminal acts. In the law, responsibility is best understood by what it excludes. One who is not responsible (liable) for their criminal act has a partial or complete excuse. A complete excuse, making one wholly nonculpable, has required, since at least the 19th century, that one not know what one is doing, not know it is wrong, or not be able to control one’s actions (be under the influence of an irresistible impulse). Under these definitions, most people serving life sentences are probably responsible for their acts. Growing up in environments of deprivation increases the likelihood that a person will go on to commit crimes But a broader understanding of responsibility is essential. For one thing, the insanity defence is a complete excuse that renders a person legally innocent (which doesn’t mean they walk free; they are likely to be institutionalised in a psychiatric facility). But there are many more whose responsibility is diminished, even though not entirely absent. The Death Penalty Information Center in its 2021 report found that: [A]ll but one of the 11 people executed in 2021 had one or more significant impairments, including: evidence of mental illness; brain injury, developmental brain damage, or an IQ in the intellectually disabled range; or chronic serious childhood trauma, neglect, and/or abuse. text:
In the quiet recesses of my mind, thoughts swirl like storm clouds gathering in a twilight sky. The topic at hand, no less daunting than the titan Atlas himself, is the ethics of climate change and our obligations to those yet unborn. The enormity of this task weighs heavily upon me, like a leaden burden dragging down my spirit.

I find myself teetering on the edge of an abyss, gazing into the darkness below. The abyss is not merely an abstract concept but a very real precipice that separates us from our future selves - those who will inherit this world we have shaped and molded. It is here where I must confront the hard truths, the uncomfortable questions, and the lingering doubts that haunt my every waking moment.

What right do we have to plunder the earth, to ravage its beauty and lay waste to its wonders, when the price of our excesses will be borne by those yet unborn? Who are we to leave behind a legacy of destruction and despair, a world ravaged by our insatiable hunger for progress and prosperity?

I find myself grappling with these questions as I walk along the shores of my beloved lake, the sun setting behind me in a blaze of fiery reds and oranges. The sight of the water, once clear and pristine, now clouded by algae blooms and littered with plastic waste, serves as a stark reminder of our failure to act responsibly.

As I stand there, the wind whipping at my back, I cannot help but think of my own children, my grandchildren, and the generations that will follow them. What kind of world have we left for them? Will they be able to enjoy the simple pleasures that I have known, or will they be forced to fight for survival in a world ravaged by our folly?

In this moment of doubt and uncertainty, I am reminded of a conversation I had with my father, many years ago. We sat on the porch, watching as the sun dipped below the horizon, and he told me about his childhood, growing up in a small town nestled among the mountains. He spoke of the clean air, the clear skies, and the abundance of wildlife that once thrived in those hills. I couldn't help but feel a pang of sadness, knowing that such memories are now but a distant dream for my own children.

But it is not enough to simply mourn the loss of what was; we must take action to prevent further destruction. We must work tirelessly to find solutions to the challenges posed by climate change, to ensure that our children and grandchildren have a future worth living. It will not be easy, for the task before us is monumental, but it is one that we cannot afford to shirk.

As I walk away from the lake, the wind at my back, I am filled with a renewed sense of purpose. I know that there are no easy answers, no quick fixes to this complex problem. But I also know that I must do everything in my power to leave behind a better world for those who will follow me.

For it is not enough to simply exist; we must strive to create, to build, and to nurture a future worth living. And in doing so, we honor not only ourselves but also the generations yet unborn. The battle may be long and difficult, but I know that together, we can rise to meet this challenge and secure a brighter, more sustainable future for all.

text: their ability’ became the tacit watchword, a neat variant of the Marxist injunction ‘to each according to their need’. The first aptitude-testers promoted the idea that each person had an innate, more-or-less fixed intellectual capacity. In the context of the early 20th century’s growing eugenics movement, the tests were often deployed to justify widespread racial discrimination. Terman claimed that what he called borderline deficient scores on the Stanford-Binet were ‘very, very common among Spanish-Indian and Mexican families of the Southwest and also among Negroes’. ‘Children of this group should be segregated into separate classes,’ he wrote in 1916. ‘They cannot master abstractions but they can often be made into efficient workers … From a eugenic point of view they constitute a grave problem because of their unusually prolific breeding.’ In Terman’s mind, then, low IQ scores were simply and unarguably the result of objective deficiency. We now understand just how wrong that notion was. Today, many psychologists understand IQ and aptitude tests to be ‘culture-bound’ to one degree or another – that is, they evaluate abilities prized in the dominant Western culture, such as sorting items into categories, and can privilege those raised in that milieu. Such inequities have persisted despite attempts to make the tests fairer to those from non-dominant cultures. As the US marinated in social Darwinism after the First World War, the government began devising its own sinister solution to the ‘grave problem’ of which Terman had warned. The US Supreme Court case Buck v Bell in 1927 ruled for compulsory sterilisation of the ‘feeble-minded’ in the name of public welfare. For more than four decades thereafter, US states sterilised thousands of people with low IQ scores; a disproportionate number of victims were nonwhite. In later years, though aptitude tests’ eugenic roots would fade from view, the ranking of test-takers according to perceived social value would continue unabated. Though I didn’t fully grasp it until well into adulthood, I was a direct beneficiary of the test-driven hierarchy that Terman worked so hard to establish. As smarts go, I was never in Beatrice Carter’s league, but my 1980s aptitude test scores were good enough to earn me the label ‘gifted’. Title: A Hidden Gem on My Lunch Break - El Tacos Locos 

Hey fellow foodies! I've stumbled upon a little gem tucked away in the heart of the city, and I simply can't keep it to myself. It's called El Tacos Locos, and let me tell you, it's been an absolute game-changer for my lunchtime routine.

I found this place on a particularly hectic weekday, when the usual suspects were packed with people waiting in lines that seemed never-ending. I was feeling hangry (yes, I've officially embraced the term), and as I turned down an unassuming alleyway, there it was - El Tacos Locos, a vibrant hole-in-the-wall taco truck.

The atmosphere here is casual yet inviting, with the aroma of freshly cooked meats wafting through the air. The line moved swiftly, thanks to the friendly server, Juan, who had an infectious smile and a knack for remembering orders. I placed my order and found a spot at one of the communal tables, watching as the team inside worked together like a well-oiled machine.

I ordered their popular Taco Al Pastor, which came with juicy, spit-grilled pork, and the Baja Fish Tacos, because who can resist a good catch? The tacos were served on warm corn tortillas, topped with a generous helping of fresh cilantro, diced onions, and a tangy lime wedge. My taste buds did a little dance as I took my first bite - the flavors were perfectly balanced, and the textures were spot-on.

Now, let's talk about the extras. I opted for their house-made guacamole, which was creamy and bursting with fresh avocado flavor. And don't even get me started on the Elotes Rojos - grilled corn slathered in a smoky mayo, sprinkled with cotija cheese, and finished off with a dusting of chili powder. It was love at first bite, I tell ya!

As I savored my meal, I couldn't help but overhear the conversations around me. El Tacos Locos seems to be a favorite among locals, and it's easy to see why - this place is a testament to authentic Mexican cuisine done right.

In a world where fast food has become the norm for busy weekdays, it's refreshing to find a hidden gem like El Tacos Locos. I'll definitely be back, and I highly recommend giving it a try if you're in the area. Just remember - don't let its humble appearance fool you; this little taco truck packs a punch!

Oh, and one last thing - if you're feeling adventurous, ask Juan about the daily specials. He'll be more than happy to share some stories and recommendations! 

Happy eating, friends!

**SEOUL: BOEING STRUGGLES WITH ACTIVIST INVESTORS AS CONSUMERS WAIT FOR CHANGE**

In the bustling streets of Seoul, South Korea, a noticeable murmur has taken over coffee shops and electronics stores - Boeing. The American aerospace giant is facing turbulent waters with its shareholders as activist investors call for change in the company's leadership and management strategies.

Boeing's struggle with activist investors began earlier this year when hedge fund JANA Partners, led by veteran investor Barry Rosenstein, announced a multi-billion dollar stake in the company. JANA is pushing for a series of changes, including the removal of current CEO David Calhoun and the appointment of a new board member with deep experience in turnaround situations.

The call for change comes amidst Boeing's ongoing recovery from the 737 MAX crisis, which saw two fatal crashes killing hundreds of passengers. The grounding of the 737 MAX fleet has had a significant impact on the company's bottom line and reputation.

Consumer reactions to this news have been mixed. Some expressed skepticism, questioning whether the new leadership would bring about the necessary change that Boeing needs. "I'm not optimistic," said Park Joon-mo, a 46-year-old businessman in Seoul who has flown on Boeing planes for over a decade. "The problems with the 737 MAX are just one part of the issues they face. They need to address their overall strategy and culture."

Others, however, voiced hope that this push from activist investors could finally bring about change in the company. "I think it's a good thing," said Kim So-jung, a 28-year-old flight attendant. "Boeing needs to do better, not just for its shareholders, but also for its customers and employees."

As the debate rages on, Boeing continues to grapple with the fallout from the 737 MAX crisis. The company has reported a net loss of $8.4 billion in the second quarter of this year, the worst quarterly loss in its history. The Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) only recently approved the return of the 737 MAX to service.

The activist investors' push for change could have far-reaching implications not just for Boeing, but also for the broader aerospace industry. If successful, it could serve as a precedent for other companies facing similar challenges. However, if unsuccessful, it could further erode consumer confidence in Boeing and the aviation industry as a whole.

In the meantime, consumers like Park Joon-mo and Kim So-jung can only wait and see what the future holds for Boeing. "I hope they get their act together," said Park. "I want to fly on a Boeing plane again without worrying about my safety."

As for Boeing, it remains to be seen whether the push from activist investors will bring about the change that the company needs to regain consumer trust and return to profitability. One thing is certain - the road ahead is fraught with challenges.

But it dissolves quickly as you descend, returning to the reassurance of tarmacked roads, streetlamps and Japan’s ubiquitous 24-hour convenience stores. Title: A Veggie's Delight at Pie Town! 

Just dropped by this brand new joint, Pie Town, the other day and lemme tell ya, it was bustling! The aroma of fresh dough and melted cheese wafting through the air had me buzzing with excitement. As a vegetarian, I've had my fair share of mediocre pizza experiences, but not today, folks!

As I stepped inside, I was greeted by the warmest welcome from the friendly staff. They were all hustling and bustling, making pizzas like a well-oiled machine, but still found time to ask about my day and assure me they had some fantastic vegetarian options. I was impressed!

I decided on the "Garden Glory" - a pizza loaded with fresh veggies like bell peppers, mushrooms, spinach, and artichoke hearts. And let me tell you, it did NOT disappoint. The crust was perfectly crispy, yet soft in the middle, and the sauce had just the right amount of zing to it. The cheese...oh, the cheese! It was gooey and delicious without being overwhelming.

Now, here comes the minor contradiction - I'm usually not a fan of dairy-free cheeses, but they managed to create a non-dairy blend that tasted remarkably close to the real deal. It melted beautifully and added just the right amount of creaminess without weighing down the pizza. Kudos to them for that!

The standout ingredient, though, was undoubtedly the veggies. They were fresh, vibrant, and full of flavor. I could taste each one distinctly, which is something I don't always get at other places. Plus, they didn't skimp on portions - I left feeling satisfied but not stuffed.

To top it all off (no pun intended), the service was prompt and friendly. The staff was attentive to my needs as a vegetarian and even suggested some tweaks to make the pizza even more delicious. By the time I left, I felt like I had made new friends!

So, if you're looking for a fantastic pizza spot that caters to both carnivores and herbivores alike, Pie Town is the place to be. Trust me, it won't disappoint! Can't wait to go back and try out their other vegetarian options. Five stars all the way!

**MEXICO CITY:**

A seismic shift in the global energy sector has been set in motion with the launch of an innovative product that promises to redefine clean energy production. The groundbreaking announcement came from SolTech Innovations, a leading renewable energy company based in Mexico City.

The new product, christened as 'EnerGem,' is a solar-powered energy storage system designed to address the intermittency issue that has long plagued the solar industry. By harnessing advanced photocatalytic technology, EnerGem can store solar energy for up to seven days without any loss of efficiency - a significant leap forward in the realm of renewable energy storage.

The implications of this breakthrough are far-reaching. According to SolTech's CEO, Maria Elena Martinez, EnerGem could potentially power an average household for an entire week on just one day's worth of sunlight. This is a game-changer for remote communities where grid connectivity is scarce and expensive, as well as disaster-prone areas frequently hit by power outages.

Moreover, the widespread adoption of EnerGem could significantly reduce carbon emissions, contributing to the global fight against climate change. According to the International Energy Agency (IEA), renewable energy needs to expand at an unprecedented rate to meet the world's growing energy demand while keeping global warming below 1.5 degrees Celsius. With EnerGem's extended storage capacity, solar power could become a more reliable and competitive source of energy, accelerating the transition towards a cleaner future.

However, the path to implementation is fraught with challenges. While SolTech has been successful in demonstrating EnerGem's efficiency in lab conditions, large-scale field testing is yet to be carried out. Additionally, the cost of production remains high, although SolTech assures that they are working diligently to bring down costs through economies of scale and technological advancements.

The global energy sector is closely watching this development with bated breath. If successful, EnerGem could usher in a new era of sustainable energy production, making clean power more accessible and affordable for all. text:
In the quiet corners of my mind, where thoughts often wander like leaves drifting through an autumn breeze, I find myself pondering the intricate dance between genes and personality traits. It's a question that has long fascinated me, a simple, everyday observation spurring an exploration into the depths of human nature.

It was during a family gathering, amidst the cacophony of laughter and the comforting aroma of grandmother's apple pie, that I noticed it. The familiar glint in my father's eyes as he shared a joke, the same spark that flickered in mine, and mirrored in my brother's. It was then that I began to wonder: Is this laughter, this shared humor, something we inherited?

As I delved deeper into the labyrinth of scientific literature, I stumbled upon a wealth of studies suggesting just that. Traits such as extraversion, conscientiousness, agreeableness, and opennessthe Big Five personality dimensionsare believed to have a significant genetic component. Yet, it's not as simple as flipping a switch or inheriting a single gene.

No, the dance between genes and personality is more like a waltz, a complex interplay of nature and nurture. Genes provide the blueprint, the basic template for our psychological makeup, but it's the environmentthe experiences, the interactions, the lessons learnedthat shapes the final form.

Consider the gene responsible for the production of dopamine, a neurotransmitter linked to reward and pleasure. Variants of this gene have been associated with extraversion, yet an extroverted personality does not mean one will seek out pleasurable experiences at all costs. Rather, it's about how these genes interact with our environment, shaping the way we respond to rewards and the risks we take.

This realization left me in awe of the complexity of our genetic inheritance, of the delicate balance between nature and nurture that shapes who we are. I found myself wondering: If our personalities are so intricately linked to our genes, what does this mean for our choices, our decisions, our very identities?

As I ponder these questions, I find myself at a crossroads. On one hand, the knowledge that my personality traits have roots in my genetic makeup offers a sense of understanding, a foundation from which to build. On the other, it raises questions about free will, about the extent to which we are truly in control of our actions and choices.

In the end, I am left with more questions than answers, standing at the edge of a vast, unexplored landscape. But this journey into the heart of genetics and personality has been enlightening, shedding light on the complex interplay between our genes and ourselves. And as I continue to explore these depths, I am filled with a sense of wonder, of awe at the intricate dance that shapes who we are.

Title: A Veggie's Delight in the Hip Jungle  But Don't Mind the Monkey Wrench! 

Star Rating: 

Hey Yelp community, it's your friendly neighborhood plant-powered foodie here! I recently found myself in the midst of a bustling crowd on a Friday night, drawn like a moth to a flame by the allure of a trendy new eatery called "Jungle Chow". Being a vegetarian, I was intrigued by its boasted plant-based offerings and decided to give it a whirl.

Upon entering, I was greeted by an atmospheric jungle-inspired decor - dim lighting, lush greenery, and the soft hum of distant tribal drums. The vibe was on point, giving off that cool, hipster ambiance that's oh-so-popular these days. However, I couldn't shake this nagging feeling that something wasn't quite right - a bit like the eerie echo of a monkey wrench in an otherwise well-oiled machine.

I settled into my table and perused the menu, which was chock-full of tantalizing plant-based options. I decided to go for the "Jungle Jackfruit Tacos" - three plump, tender jackfruit tacos with a zesty mango salsa, served on fresh corn tortillas. The presentation was beautiful, and the first bite sent my taste buds into an enchanting dance! The flavors were well-balanced, and the textures were delightfully satisfying.

However, here's where things get a little... complicated. While I was enjoying my meal, I couldn't help but notice that the background music felt like it was playing on two different speeds - one part fast, the other slow. It was almost as if someone had forgotten to reset their turntable after their lunchtime mix!

Moving on from the discordant rhythm, I must give a shoutout to the packaging quality for takeout/leftovers. My tacos arrived in eco-friendly containers that were not only sturdy but also easy to reheat at home. A small detail, perhaps, but one that shows thoughtfulness and care.

In conclusion, Jungle Chow is a place where the vegetarian/vegan community can find delightful plant-based options in an atmospheric setting. However, I found myself slightly underwhelmed by the inconsistencies in the overall experience - from the out-of-sync background music to the mysterious monkey wrench that left me questioning what else may be amiss.

Would I return? Yes, I would, but with a grain of salt and an open mind. I hope that they can iron out the kinks and deliver on the promise of a truly immersive dining experience for all. Until then, fellow veggie friends, I invite you to explore Jungle Chow - just remember, be prepared for a few surprises along the way!

text:
In the bustling heart of London, where the aroma of fresh-baked bread wafts from a quaint corner bakery and the rhythmic hum of conversations punctuates the air, I find myself pondering over the enduring influence of Karl Marx, a man who, 170 years after his death, continues to stir the political landscape like an unyielding gust of wind.

Marx's ideas, born from the tumultuous industrial revolution, were a critique of the capitalist system that was then taking root. His work, "Das Kapital," presented a profound analysis of the economic forces driving society. Yet, it was his concept of class struggle and the quest for social justice that resonated deeply, transcending the boundaries of time and place.

Fast forward to the 21st century, and we find ourselves grappling with similar issues. The widening gap between the rich and the poor, the exploitation of labor, and the pursuit of profits over people are concerns that echo Marx's times. The Occupy Wall Street movement, for instance, was a potent reminder of the ongoing class struggle, albeit in a different context.

But it's not just the left-leaning politics that bear the imprint of Marx. Even the right-wing conservatism, with its emphasis on individual freedom and limited government intervention, can be traced back to Marx's critique of state control. The capitalist system, in its relentless pursuit of growth and efficiency, often reflects the "ruthless" competition that Marx described.

Marx's ideas have also shaped academic discourse. Critical theory, a school of thought within sociology and cultural studies, seeks to expose and challenge power structures in society. Marx's emphasis on the role of ideology in maintaining the status quo remains a cornerstone of this approach.

However, the application of Marx's ideas is not without controversy. Critics argue that his prediction of the inevitable collapse of capitalism has not materialized, and his solutions for a just society seem idealistic at best. Yet, his work continues to provoke thought and inspire debate, making him as relevant today as he was in the 19th century.

As I walk past the bakery, the clanging of pots and the smell of fresh bread serve as reminders of the ongoing struggle between labor and capital, between profit and people. The legacy of Marx looms large over this struggle, a testament to the enduring power of his ideas in shaping our understanding of the world and our pursuit of a more just society.

Yet, the journey is far from over. As we grapple with the complexities of the 21st century, Marx's ideas offer valuable insights, but they are not the final answers. They serve as a compass, guiding us through the labyrinth of power, exploitation, and struggle, reminding us that the pursuit of justice is an ongoing journey, one that requires vigilance, courage, and resilience.

Title: A Sunny Afternoon at the Silver Screen Cinema  A Mixed Bag

As for the movie itself (I won't spoil anything), it was entertaining enough, but not exactly groundbreaking. I found myself wishing for a more thought-provoking plot or at least better character development. However, it did serve its purpose of providing a few hours of escapism on a beautiful day.

Here's where things get a bit tricky - the seating situation. I usually enjoy sitting in the back row, but today there were some technical issues with the sound system that made it difficult to hear clearly. I ended up moving closer to the screen, which unfortunately blocked my view of the outdoor scenery. A minor inconvenience, but something worth noting if you plan on visiting for an outdoor screening.

On the plus side, the staff were quick to address the sound issue and promised a fix for future showings. They also offer blankets for those chilly evenings, which is a nice touch.

In conclusion, I'd give Silver Screen Cinema a 3-star rating. The reasonable prices, decent food options, and outdoor screen make it an appealing choice, but the sound issues during my visit and the less-than-stellar movie selection bring it down a notch. text: Conventional wisdom depicts moral struggle as an internal conflict between a higher moral self and an untamed dark side. This picture pervades popular imagination: the angel and the devil on either shoulder, the ‘two wolf’ parable, the Ego and the Id, the ‘true self’ and the ‘false self’. It resonates with religious traditions that place us between angels and animals in a Great Chain of Being, leaving us torn between higher and lower, spirit and body, good and evil, the demands of conscience and the lure of sin. This view also calls to mind a philosophical tradition from Plato to Immanuel Kant that often presents life’s major moral struggles as a kind of combat between the requirements of duty and the dangers of desire. The self is fragmented and must struggle for wholeness by casting out or silencing its evil components, refusing to give immoral intentions a foothold in thought and deed. A good deal of moral theory, therefore, tends to assume that there’s a morally right answer about what one ought to do in any given circumstance. Any difficulty in doing the right thing results from (evil, selfish) resistance, not from the fact that one cannot do all the good or valuable things that one is called upon to do. However, this familiar view ignores the fact that, in many cases, the problem is not how best to override or silence one’s dark side, but how to cope with having too many good or morally neutral demands on your limited time, energy or resources. In other words, the key issue in many cases is not whether to be moral at all – but rather how best to distribute your moral resources in conditions of scarcity and conflict. Coping well with this latter kind of moral challenge requires very different ways of thinking about moral agency and how to lead good lives. There are (at least) three different classes of goods that regularly give rise to incommensurable but competing legitimate moral claims, each revealed through a different practical stance that we adopt towards the world as we try to figure out what to do and who to be. On this picture, each agent is indeed fragmented, but this fragmentation is not best understood as an internal conflict between ‘good’ and ‘evil’ selves. Instead, moral conflict should be understood in terms of competing dimensions of the good – not all of which can be accommodated in any given moment. What are these three basic normative domains or classes of value? It can be helpful to think of these in terms of the traditional literary distinction between the first-, second- and third-person perspectives. A novel written from the first-person perspective provides access to the protagonist’s struggles from the inside; the reader says ‘I’ along with her. In the second-person perspective, the focus is on the other person: the ‘you’ takes centre stage.

text: Many academic fields can be said to ‘study morality’. Of these, the philosophical sub-discipline of normative ethics studies morality in what is arguably the least alienated way. Rather than focusing on how people and societies think and talk about morality, normative ethicists try to figure out which things are, simply, morally good or bad, and why. The philosophical sub-field of meta-ethics adopts, naturally, a ‘meta-’ perspective on the kinds of enquiry that normative ethicists engage in. It asks whether there are objectively correct answers to these questions about good or bad, or whether ethics is, rather, a realm of illusion or mere opinion. Most of my work in the past decade has been in meta-ethics. I believe that there are truths about what’s morally right and wrong. I believe that some of these truths are objective or, as they say in the literature, ‘stance-independent’. That is to say, it’s not my or our disapproval that makes torture morally wrong; torture is wrong because, to put it simply, it hurts people a lot. I believe that these objective moral truths are knowable, and that some people are better than others are at coming to know them. You can even call them ‘moral experts’ if you wish. Of course, not everyone agrees with all of that. Some are simply confused; they conflate ‘objective’ with ‘culturally universal’ or ‘innate’ or ‘subsumable under a few exceptionless principles’ or some such. But many people’s misgivings about moral objectivity are more clear headed and deeper. In particular, I find that some demur because they think that, for there to be moral truths, let alone objective, knowable ones, morality would have to have a kind of ‘foundation’ that, in their view, is nowhere to be found. Others, anxious to help, try to show that there’s a firm foundation or ultimate ground for morality after all. It’s my view that both sides of this conflict are off on the wrong foot. Morality is objective, but it neither requires nor admits of a foundation. It just kind of floats there, along with the evaluative realm more generally, unsupported by anything else. Parts of it can be explained by other parts, but the entirety of the web or network of good and evil is brute. Maybe you think that’s weird and even worthy of outright dismissal. I once thought the same thing. The purpose of this essay, which is based on my book Pragmatist Quietism: A Meta-Ethical System (2022), is to encourage you to start seeing this aspect of the world as I now see it. The first question we should ask is: what exactly is a ‘foundation’? We can get clearer on what a foundation is by querying whether a moral theory like utilitarianism might count as one. In a move that has sent ripples through the tech industry, NVIDIA Corporation, the world's leading manufacturer of graphics processing units (GPUs), has announced its intent to merge with British chipmaker Arm Holdings. This merger, if successful, would create a powerhouse in the semiconductor market, combining NVIDIA's expertise in GPU technology with Arm's dominant position in microprocessors used in mobile devices and internet-of-things (IoT) devices.

The news has left investors scrambling to digest the implications of this unexpected merger. Shares of both companies saw a significant surge immediately following the announcement, with NVIDIA stocks rising by 13.6% at the close of trading on Friday. Arm Holdings, however, remains silent on its stance regarding the proposed deal, leaving investors in suspense.

Analysts predict that this merger could redefine the landscape of the semiconductor industry. The combined entity is expected to generate a staggering $250 billion in revenue annually, making it a formidable competitor to tech giants like Intel and Qualcomm. The synergies between NVIDIA's AI capabilities and Arm's energy-efficient designs could also lead to groundbreaking innovations in areas such as autonomous vehicles, data centers, and 5G infrastructure.

However, concerns have been raised about the potential monopolistic implications of this merger. Critics argue that by consolidating market leaders in both GPU and microprocessor sectors, the merged entity could exert excessive control over the semiconductor industry. This could result in higher prices for consumers and limited choices for manufacturers.

In response to these concerns, NVIDIA's CEO, Jensen Huang, assured investors that the company remains committed to maintaining a competitive market. He emphasized that Arm's intellectual property (IP) licensing model would continue post-merger, ensuring fair access to technology for all potential customers.

As the dust settles and more details emerge about this proposed merger, one thing is certain: this is a significant move for both NVIDIA and Arm Holdings, and its consequences will be felt throughout the tech industry. Investors will continue to closely monitor developments surrounding this merger, hoping to capitalize on any opportunities that may arise from this potential game-changer in the world of semiconductors.

Meanwhile, it remains to be seen whether Arm Holdings will choose to embrace or resist this proposed union. As the clock ticks down towards a decision deadline set for later this year, all eyes are on Arm and its future with NVIDIA. Only time will tell if this merger becomes a reality, reshaping the tech industry as we know it.

Ah, the intricate dance between biology and human behavior, a labyrinthine web that I find myself forever entangled in. It's a topic that leaves me both fascinated and skeptical, like a magpie drawn to shiny trinkets, yet always questioning their true worth.

Let's delve into this tangled thicket, shall we? Genes, those snippets of DNA that make us who we are, play a significant role in shaping our behavior, don't they? Yet, how much of our actions can truly be attributed to them? Can we really boil down the complexity of human behavior to a string of As, Ts, Cs, and Gs?

Take emotions, for instance. Are they merely chemical reactions in our brain, or are they something more profound, something beyond our genetic makeup? I find myself torn between these two perspectives. On one hand, emotions seem to be hardwired into our biological framework, triggered by hormones and neural pathways. On the other, they appear to be as changeable and fluid as the wind, shaped by experiences, culture, and environment.

And then there's learning, a process that transforms us from mere genetic copies into unique individuals. We absorb information, we adapt, we evolve. But is this all just a complex dance of neurons firing in our brains? Or is there a deeper, less tangible force at work?

I can't help but feel a pang of doubt when I consider these questions. The more I learn, the more I realize how little I truly understand. It's like peeling back layers of an onion, only to find another layer beneath it. And each layer raises more questions than it answers.

But here's where things get interesting, I think. While biology may provide the foundation for our behavior, it is our experiences, our choices, and our environment that build upon this foundation, creating something unique and complex. We are not mere slaves to our genes, but rather architects of our own destinies.

So, as I stand at the crossroads of biology and human behavior, I am left with more questions than answers. But isn't that the beauty of exploration? The thrill of the unknown, the tantalizing promise of discovery? I, for one, can't wait to continue this journey, to delve deeper into this labyrinthine dance between nature and nurture.

text:
Hey there, friend,

I've been thinking about us, haven't I? About the grand tapestry we weave together, our collective struggle for justice and equality. It's a heavy thought, isn't it? The challenge of enforcing human rights globally feels like trying to hold back an ocean with a leaky bucket.

I mean, just look at the news. Wars raging in far-off lands, refugees fleeing unimaginable horrors, and children dying from hunger and preventable diseases. And yet, here we are, sitting comfortably in our homes, scrolling through the misery on our screens, feeling helpless and overwhelmed.

But, hey, don't let that discourage you. Remember, every great change started small - a whisper that grew into a roar, a single step that turned into a march. We can make a difference, one small act at a time.

I've been reading up on this, you know. About how some countries are making strides in human rights while others are sliding backwards. It's frustrating, isn't it? How some leaders prioritize their own power over the well-being of their people. But then I remember the heroes who fought against all odds, who stood up for what was right even when everyone else turned away.

So, let's not forget them. Let's not give up hope. Because if we do, who will carry on their legacy? If we turn our backs now, who will stand up when it matters most?

I don't have all the answers, friend. I'm just as lost and confused as you are. But I know this much - that we can't give up. We can't let fear or frustration win. We have to keep moving forward, keep pushing for change, keep demanding justice for all.

Because in the end, isn't that what it's all about? Isn't it about ensuring every single person on this planet is treated with dignity and respect, regardless of where they were born or who they are? Isn't it about creating a world where everyone has the chance to live a life free from fear, free from oppression, free from poverty?

I believe we can do it. I believe in us, friend. And I hope you do too. Let's keep pushing, let's keep fighting, and let's never give up on our shared vision of a better world. Because that, my friend, is the only way to truly honor the heroes who came before us.

Until next time, take care, and remember - we are stronger together.

Yours,
A fellow traveler on this journey called life.

Mount Holyoke became an important outpost for French culture in the US during the war. At gatherings of exiled scholars organised by Wahl, Bespaloff met Jacques Maritain, André Masson, Marc Chagall and Claude Lévi-Strauss. This ‘small, dark lady who wore white gloves’, as her translator Mary McCarthy described her, also made an impression on Hannah Arendt who visited in August 1944 to deliver a lecture on Franz Kafka. Arendt’s reading of Kafka, later published in Partisan Review, echoed Bespaloff’s existentialist despair. Under the dark shadow of war, Arendt describes humanity as inescapably trapped in history’s meshes. Kafka’s ‘nightmare of a world’ had become reality. In an essay on Camus, her last published work, Bespaloff describes how history forced her generation ‘to live in a climate of violent death’. After the war, despite previously having been fêted by them, Bespaloff became a vocal critic of the new generation of French existentialists, especially Sartre. In a 1946 letter to the musicologist Boris de Schloezer, Bespaloff wrote that ‘the hollowness of subjectivity that Sartre opposes to what I call “magical interiority” is much less the foundation of a new humanism than the harbinger of a new conformity.’ She argued that, instead of liberating the individual, Sartre’s existentialism destroyed the magical interiority through which humans can authentically connect with one another. For Bespaloff, Sartre degraded the subject into an object under the gaze of the Other. This objectified ‘subjectivity curiously aligns with American “individualism”, which unleashes itself in action to mask the absence of the individual.’ Like Helen’s Troy, the US felt both dull and hostile to Bespaloff. Bespaloff’s journey to Mount Holyoke was her final exile. During term break, in April 1949, for reasons not entirely clear, she sealed her kitchen doors and turned on the gas oven. Her own complex fugue ended with a tragic cadence. She had written earlier of the happiness that can be found in an instant. In her final note, alluding to Camus’s claim, she wrote: ‘One can imagine Sisyphus happy, but joy is forever out of his reach.’

In pondering the intricate tapestry of Margaret Atwood's dystopian narratives, I find myself drawn to the profound insights they offer about our contemporary social anxieties. These works, woven with threads of fear, uncertainty, and a keen awareness of our precarious relationship with nature, seem to be both mirror and prophecy.

Take, for instance, 'The Handmaid's Tale.' Set in the oppressive Republic of Gilead, this story mirrors our deep-seated fears about gender inequality and loss of reproductive autonomy. The Handmaid's plight reflects a collective unease that women's rights could be rolled back to prehistoric levels, where our bodies are not our own but commodities for the state or patriarchy.

Similarly, 'Oryx and Crake' warns us about unchecked scientific advancement and corporate greed. The catastrophic consequences of creating genetically modified organisms, especially the Crake's tragic creationthe Snowmanserve as a cautionary tale against tampering with nature without due consideration for its fragile balance.

Yet, it's not just about fear-mongering or doomsday prophecies. Atwood's works subtly challenge us to consider our actions, inviting introspection and reflection on how we shape the world around us. For me, this resonates deeply when I think of my own footprintthe small daily choices that add up to significant environmental impact.

One example from my life is my reliance on single-use plastics. Every time I purchase a bottle of water or use a plastic straw, I feel a pang of guilt, knowing that these items could end up in our oceans, causing harm to marine life and eventually making their way back to our dinner tables. It's unsettling to realize the interconnectedness of our actions, not just with each other but also with the natural world.

Atwood's dystopias force us to confront these uncomfortable truths about ourselves and society. They hold up a dark mirror, revealing our ugliest fears and reminding us that complacency leads to disaster. These stories challenge us to change, encouraging us to question our beliefs, reexamine our actions, and strive for a better worldone where humanity lives in harmony with nature rather than exploiting it for short-term gain.

As I delve deeper into Atwood's works, I find myself compelled not only by the gripping narratives but also by their profound messages about our shared responsibility to protect and preserve this fragile planet. We are creatures of culture – we are indefatigable innovators, and we like to copy what those around us are doing The final workhorse of gesture is also the most abstract: the metaphors that people create with their hands. Many of the metaphors humans seem to favour are based on spatial images, and so they lend themselves well to the imagistic medium of gesture. The best-studied example of gestural metaphors are those used to represent time. People all over the world think of time as having concrete spatial properties. And they gesture accordingly, as though events had a certain ‘length’ or were located in different places. English speakers, for example, often imagine sequences either as a line stretching across their bodies – with past events to the left and future ones to the right – or as a path they are walking on – with past events behind them and future ones ahead. These particular metaphorical models, however, are not universal. Speakers of Aymara in the Andes point to a future that is not ahead but behind them. Mandarin speakers locate next week below last week. When Yupno speakers gesture about time, as my collaborators and I have documented, they locate the past downhill and the future uphill, no matter which way they are facing. A complete account of gestural universals and diversity would have to consider a number of other phenomena, too – how people use their hands and bodies to greet each other, to represent numbers, to ask questions, to beckon or dismiss, and to negotiate the conversational floor. Certainly, there is still a lot of work to be done to further document such behaviours. But a reasonable guess is that the punchline would be the same: gestures around the world exhibit constrained diversity. This, after all, is the primary moral that can be extracted from the best-studied workhorses of gestural communication. On the one hand, several general methods of gesturing – signalling ‘yes’ and ‘no’, pointing, depicting, conjuring metaphors in the air – appear to be universally used and in constrained ways. On the other, when we dig into the details of precisely how such methods are deployed, we find unexpected texture. This recurring pattern of constrained diversity should perhaps be unsurprising. People everywhere have the same bodily equipment to work with – our hands, arms and heads are basically the same, and they flex, bend and rotate in the same basic ways. We also have the same kinds of minds, which means we tend to notice and care about the same sorts of things. And we all have to solve similar communication problems – problems such as orienting attention, correcting misunderstandings, and posing questions. text: and what is going on. Humans are not simply social beings but political ones. From playgrounds to corridors of power, they are constantly forming, modifying, ending and generally complicating alliances. Stable cues that signal alliance, such as speech traits or styles of dress, offer regularities that help people orient themselves in the social whirl. Race might be one such cue, but Cosmides, Tooby and Kurzban’s experiments from 2001 show that it does not necessarily carry the weight its salience as a social issue would suggest. People do indeed seem to categorise each other by race automatically. Even so, the experimenters were able to downgrade it as a category in a matter of minutes by using neutral colours as markers. When people were told that individuals wearing grey or yellow shirts belonged to two groups, they relied much more heavily on the shirts than the ethnic appearance of the wearers to decide which group they belonged to. Other researchers have reported that accent also trumps race. Young children choosing friends seem to prefer children of other races who speak with the same accent to children of the same race who speak with a different one. Those American findings might seem familiar to people living in Britain, where the Union flag erased visible ethnic differences between the Olympic athletes of Team GB last summer, and young Londoners of all kinds of heritage share an accent peculiar to their singularly diverse city. Although people are hazy about what Britishness is, the vast majority of them no longer think it depends on race. At the same time, many of them mistrust Muslims and consider immigrants unwelcome. The great British in-group has reinvented both itself and its others. It is a much fairer nation than it was, inclusive to a degree unimaginable a generation or two ago. But in recomposing its coalitions, it inevitably sets up the potential for new biases and unfairness. Perhaps we simply have to accept that the relationship between us and them is always a work in progress.

text:
In the vast landscape of contemporary artificial intelligence (AI) discourse, one name echoes with remarkable persistence: Alan Turing. The British mathematician, logician, and pioneer in computer science, whose groundbreaking work on mathematical machines and computational theory laid the very foundations for our modern understanding of AI, continues to cast a long, influential shadow over this burgeoning field. Yet, as we grapple with the complexities and conundrums of AI, it becomes increasingly evident that Turing's ideas about machine intelligence are not just historical footnotes but rather living, breathing ideals that shape our debates in profound ways.

Turing, famously, proposed the "Turing Test," a test to determine whether or not a machine can exhibit intelligent behavior equivalent to, or indistinguishable from, that of a human. The test, designed as a thought experiment, has become an emblematic touchstone in AI research. It raises intriguing questions about what constitutes intelligence, consciousness, and the nature of the human mindquestions that continue to bedevil us today. Is it enough for a machine to mimic human behavior to be considered intelligent? Or should we seek something deeper, more authentic, more human-like in our creations?

These questions are not merely academic exercises but rather practical dilemmas that confront AI researchers and developers daily. As we strive to create machines capable of performing tasks hitherto thought exclusive to humans, we are forced to grapple with the ethical implications of creating entities that can think, feel, and perhaps even understand the world in ways we cannot fully comprehend. Turing's ideas, therefore, serve as a kind of Rosetta Stone for navigating these uncharted waters, helping us to decipher the enigmatic language of AI and find common ground between our ambitions and our apprehensions.

Yet, one cannot help but wonder if Turing's ideas have become something of a double-edged sword. On the one hand, they serve as beacons guiding us towards an understanding of machine intelligence that is both nuanced and humane. On the other, they risk obscuring the unique qualities that distinguish AI from human intelligence, leading to unrealistic expectations and potential misuse. For instance, Turing's emphasis on mimicking human behavior may inadvertently perpetuate the notion that AI should aspire to be more like us, rather than forging its own path towards a distinct, yet complementary, form of intelligence.

Furthermore, Turing's work has played a pivotal role in shaping our understanding of the limits and potentials of AI. His insights into computational theory and the capabilitiesand limitationsof machines have profoundly influenced the trajectory of AI research, guiding us towards the creation of ever more sophisticated algorithms and architectures that push the boundaries of what is possible with machines. However, as we continue to unlock the mysteries of machine intelligence, it becomes increasingly clear that Turing's ideas, while foundational, are far from exhaustive.

In conclusionor perhaps better put, in a nod towards the unfinished, exploratory nature of this writingTuring's ideas about machine intelligence remain a powerful force shaping AI debates. They serve as guiding stars, helping us to navigate the complex landscape of AI research and development while simultaneously posing challenging questions that demand our ongoing engagement. As we continue to grapple with the implications of Turing's legacy, it is essential that we maintain an open-minded yet skeptical stance, continually questioning the assumptions that underpin our understanding of machine intelligence and seeking new insights that can help us to create machines that are not just intelligent but also beneficial to humanity.

I've been pondering over the impact of scientific revolutions on our philosophical worldviews, especially the tension these transformations seem to create between the desire for individual freedom and the longing for a sense of belonging.

You know how it is when a new scientific theory comes along and shakes up everything we thought we knew? It's like an earthquake that makes the ground beneath our feet feel unsteady, leaving us questioning what we truly believe. Take Copernicus, for instance. When he proposed that the Earth revolves around the Sun, it was a slap in the face to centuries-old beliefs about our place in the universe. It made people question their role and significance in the grand scheme of things.

But here's where it gets interesting - this newfound uncertainty opens up space for exploration, for questioning, and for redefining our worldviews. In this chaos, we find freedom to imagine and create new ideas. We are no longer bound by the constraints of what was once accepted as truth. And yet, this freedom can feel isolating, making us yearn for a sense of belonging - a shared understanding and connection with others who are navigating these shifting sands alongside us.

This tension between freedom and belonging is not new, but it seems to be amplified in the context of scientific revolutions. As we push the boundaries of knowledge and redefine our understanding of reality, we grapple with questions about our individual identities, our role in society, and our place in the cosmos. And as we struggle to reconcile these questions, we find ourselves at once free to explore new ideas, yet yearning for a sense of belonging within this rapidly evolving philosophical landscape.

text: leadership of Jawaharlal Nehru showed a commitment to some Pan-Asian principles, which retained popular appeal at the Bandung Conference of 1955. This meeting of 29 Asian and African states, comprising more than half the world’s people, was the last major expression of Asian solidarity, and was later subsumed by Cold War rivalries and nation-statebuilding projects. Pan-Islamism has also proceeded in a series of fits and starts over the past century. From Turkey and Egypt to Indonesia and Algeria, the idea of Muslim intellectualism and global Muslim solidarity empowered 20th-century nationalist leaders and movements. By the mid-1960s, the majority of the world’s Muslims had gained freedom from European colonial rule. The Turkish Parliament had abolished the Ottoman caliphate back in 1924, and by the 1950s that caliphate was almost forgotten. Nearly a fifth of the way into the 21st century, however, Pan-Africanism and Pan-Asianism seems to have vanished but Pan-Islamism and the ideal of Muslim world solidarity survives. Why? The answer lies in the final stages of the Cold War. It was in the 1980s that a new Muslim internationalism emerged, as part of a rising political Islam. It was not a clash between the primordial civilisational traditions of Islam and the West, or a reassertion of authentic religious values. It wasn’t even a persistence of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism, but rather a new formation of the Cold War. A Saudi-US alliance began promoting the idea of Muslim solidarity in the 1970s as an alternative to the secular Pan-Arabism of the Egyptian president Gamal Abdel Nasser, whose country allied with the Soviet Union. Any ideas of an ‘Islamic’ utopia would have floundered if not for the failures of many post-colonial nation-states and the subsequent public disillusionment of many Muslims. The notion that Pan-Islamism represents authentic, ancient, repressed Muslim political values in revolt against global Westernisation and secularisation was initially a paranoid obsession of Western colonial officers, but recently it comes mainly from Islamists. Western pundits and journalists have erred in accepting at face value Islamist claims about Islam’s essential political values. The kind of Islamism that’s identified with Egypt’s Muslim Brotherhood or Ruhollah Khomeini’s Iran did not exist before the 1970s. None of the Indian Muslims meeting Wilson, nor the late Ottoman-era caliphs, were interested in imposing Sharia in their society. None of them wanted to veil women. On the contrary, the first Pan-Islamist generation was highly modernist: they were proponents of the liberation of women, racial equality and cosmopolitanism. Indian Muslims, for example, were very proud that the Ottoman caliph had Greek and Armenian ministers and ambassadors. They also wanted to see the British Crown appointing Hindu and Muslim ministers and high-level officials in their governments. None would have desired or predicted the separation of Turks and Greeks in Ottoman lands, Arabs and Jews in Palestine, and Muslims and Hindus in India. Only the basic form of early 20th-century Pan-Islamism survives

After a record-breaking 2020 that saw him amass over $15 million in prize money alone, this year's earnings have plummeted by nearly half.

The slump began early in the season when Djokovic lost to his longtime rival, Rafael Nadal, in straight sets at the Australian Open. Although he managed to secure a win against Stefanos Tsitsipas in the semifinals of the Dubai Tennis Championships, it wasn't enough to shake off the mounting doubts about his form.

A source close to one of these brands revealed that they were "not pleased with the lackluster performance" and had requested a review of the endorsement deal.

Industry analysts attribute much of Djokovic's struggle to his decision to form his own management company, Joker Sport Management (JSM). The move, which took place at the height of his success in 2019, was seen as a bold step towards greater control over his career. However, critics argue that it may have come too soon, leaving little room for experienced advisors to guide him through the ups and downs of professional tennis.

"Novak's decision to manage himself was a calculated risk, but it seems it might have backfired," said sports marketing expert Dr. Sarah Williams of the University of Southern California. "He's now shoulder-deep in business decisions without the necessary expertise, and that could be affecting his performance on court."

The timing of Djokovic's dip in form couldn't be worse for JSM. With high-profile events like Wimbledon and the US Open looming, the company is under immense pressure to turn things around quickly. One potential solution lies in bringing on board a seasoned sports executive, someone with a proven track record of steering top athletes through challenging periods.

As Djokovic heads into the grass court season, fans, sponsors, and industry experts will be eagerly watching his every move. text:
In the labyrinthine world of contemporary art, one name consistently surfaces with an electrifying jolt: Marina Abramovi. Her work, often described as provocative and boundary-pushing, has left many spectators stunned, bewildered, and, at times, outright perplexed. This essay aims to navigate the complexities of her performance art and probe the subtle ways it provokes, disturbing our comfort zones while inviting us to question the very essence of human existence.

As I walked into the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) for the first time in 2010, I was greeted by an eerily silent, dimly lit space. A woman sat motionless at a table, her gaze fixed upon a glass of water. This was 'The Artist is Present,' one of Abramovi's most celebrated works, and it left me feeling unnerved and intrigued in equal measure. Why was this woman just sitting there? Was she even alive? What was I supposed to feel or think while watching her? These questions lingered long after I left the gallery, gnawing at my mind like an insatiable hunger.

Abramovi's performance art often plays with these uncomfortable sensations of disquiet and uncertainty. In 'Rhythm 0,' for instance, she laid herself bare (literally and figuratively) before her audience, arming herself with a wide array of objects  from benign items like flowers and feathers to potentially lethal ones such as a gun and a knife. The performance was left entirely in the hands of the audience, who could interact with her or the objects at their discretion. This audacious move challenged societal norms and expectations, forcing viewers to confront their own impulses and biases.

Yet, Abramovi's work transcends simple provocation and invites a deeper exploration of our relationship with ourselves, others, and the world around us. In 'The House with the Ocean View,' she underwent a series of emotionally charged interactions with strangers, each one representing someone who had been closely associated with her in her life  from lovers to family members to friends. The performance was, at its core, an exercise in vulnerability and connection, inviting us to consider the profound impact our relationships have on our lives.

It's impossible not to feel a sense of unease when confronted with Abramovi's work. Her performances seem to tap into some primal, instinctual part of ourselves that we rarely acknowledge or examine. They push us to question the boundaries between art and life, performance and reality, self and other. But what lies beneath this discomfort? Is it fear, curiosity, or something else entirely?

As I continue my exploration of Abramovi's work, I find myself grappling with these questions and more. Her performances challenge me to confront my own biases, to question the narratives I construct about myself and the world around me. They remind me that there is beauty in vulnerability, power in connection, and strength in embracing the unknown.

In the end, Abramovi's performance art serves as a mirror, reflecting back our most deeply held fears, desires, and beliefs. It invites us to peer into the abyss, to confront the darkness within ourselves and emerge changed  if not quite enlightened. In this sense, her work is not just provocative; it's transformative, offering a glimpse of what it means to be human in all its complexities, contradictions, and imperfections.

As I write these words, I find myself still grappling with the subtle ways Abramovi's performance art provokes us. Her work continues to challenge me, leaving me questioning my own beliefs about art, life, and the human experience. And it is this sense of open-endedness, this tantalizing glimpse into the unknown, that keeps me coming back for more  eager to delve deeper, to unravel the mysteries that her performances so skillfully weave.

Then in 1705 she entered the public sphere again with another work, more radical than the first, titled Occasional Thoughts in Reference to a Vertuous or Christian Life, in which she argued that women should contribute to all intellectual subjects: ‘I see no Reason why it should not be thought that all Science lyes as open to a Lady as to a Man.’ Masham was also close to the Enlightenment philosopher John Locke. Their friendship endured for almost 20 years. It was an intellectual, personal and at times romantic exchange that began before her marriage and endured after Locke moved into her home with her husband and children. No need to write letters when you can share ideas near the fire in the evenings. This was a period of great philosophical flourishing for Masham, during which she wrote her two and only books. If it were not for Masham’s affiliation with Locke, I’m certain that these two books would have been completely lost to us. In fact, she probably wouldn’t have written them. I resisted exploring her close bond with Locke when I wrote my dissertation about her ideas years ago. At the time, it seemed the right thing to do. I wanted to treat Masham’s body of work as I would have treated any male philosopher’s: independent of his private life. I eventually realised my mistake. No person’s work exists in a vacuum, and by acknowledging Masham’s relationship to Locke – he broke her heart yet encouraged her philosophical work – I gained a deeper appreciation of her oeuvre, as well as her experience of being a woman in philosophy. When I finally sat down to read her letters to Locke, I was not in a good place. I’d pursued a career in philosophy with the dream of living a life of enquiry, but I didn’t expect it to be such a boys’ club or that I’d be managing chronic self-doubt and self-censorship. As I read Masham’s correspondence, I realised that I, too, had a John Locke – that is, a man in philosophy on whom I depended for a sense of belonging in the field. It obscures our understanding of smell. It bypasses a lot of other sensory sensations, including the hidden senses of proprioception and interoception. text:
In the grand tapestry of humanity, we find ourselves woven together by threads as diverse as our dreams and fears. One such thread that binds us, often overlooked yet profoundly powerful, is art. Art, my friends, is more than just a pretty picture or a catchy tune; it's a reflection of our souls, a window into our hearts, and a mirror held up to society.

Why do we create art? Is it merely an expression of vanity, a need to make our mark upon the world, or is there something deeper at play? Perhaps it stems from our innate desire to communicate, to share our experiences, emotions, and ideas with others. Art allows us to bridge the chasm of silence and speak in a language that transcends borders, cultures, and generations.

Consider a simple painting - a splash of colors on a canvas. To some, it might be just that: colors. But to others, it could be the sunset over their childhood home, the memories of a loved one lost, or the hope for a brighter tomorrow. Art serves as a conduit for emotions, giving voice to the voiceless and offering solace to the brokenhearted.

Art also plays a crucial role in shaping our behavior. It challenges us, provokes us, and moves us. It forces us to confront uncomfortable truths about ourselves and society, encouraging introspection and empathy. The power of art lies not only in its ability to evoke powerful emotions but also in its capacity to inspire change. Artists have been instrumental in driving social movements, from protesting injustice to advocating for the marginalized.

However, the influence of art on human behavior is not always so noble. Art can reinforce harmful stereotypes and perpetuate inequality. It can reflect and amplify the ugliest aspects of our society. Yet, it also offers us a chance to challenge those very same reflections, to question the status quo, and to strive for a better world.

As I ponder these thoughts, I find myself grappling with more questions than answers. Is art a reflection of our humanity or a means to transcend it? Does it serve as a catalyst for change or a mirror held up to our darkest fears? These questions remain unanswered, their mysteries shrouded in the beauty and complexity that is art.

So, let us continue to create, to explore, and to question. Let us use art not only as an escape from reality but also as a means to engage with it more deeply. For in the end, it is through this shared journey of creation and exploration that we truly come alive.
