Emotionally, we go to greater lengths to avoid a bad mood than to experience a good one. Pessimists tend to assess their health more accurately than optimists. Our gloomy bent finds its way into spoken language, with almost two thirds of English words conveying the negative side of things. text: desires and goals: however, seeing as plants and microbes don’t obviously have these, it’s unclear what of them survives. These curious identity questions don’t yet have obvious answers, and it’s unclear what kind of ancestral relation fission creates. A lot would likely depend on the particulars of the fission involving ageing, growth and, in particular, symmetry. If there’s perfect symmetry during fission, then perhaps Parfit is correct and certain organisms pop out of existence – meaning fission is not only reproduction but also a very strange kind of death. However, if the fission isn’t symmetrical, then some organisms might be more like Lewis’s streets. If this is the case, perhaps colonies of bacteria are composed of many individuals that share a single identity. It’s clear that these kinds of fission meet Griesemer’s criteria for reproduction. But the problem is that fission doesn’t create clear links from parent to offspring: a clear, vertical lineage of descent. This creates difficulties for our notions of reproduction. To return to the questions that opened this essay, how do we define the relationships between organisms created through fission? Does this process produce clones, siblings or just two instances of the original? To see why, let’s return to the Devil’s Ivy. Say someone cuts a shoot from a mature Devil’s Ivy plant and replants the shoot in another pot, where it develops into a mature plant. According to Griesemer’s criterion, this counts as an instance of reproduction. But has the plant really reproduced in the sense of 18th-century-style reproduction? Is the original plant really a parent and the shoot its offspring? It’s far from obvious. First, remember that the shoot and the original plant are genetically identical clones. All that changes is physical connectedness, but cutting and replanting a shoot doesn’t resemble something like human birth. Other creatures are also able to persist through physical disconnectedness, like the ‘immortal’ jellyfish Turritopsis dohrnii which, depending on its environmental conditions, moves between being a physically connected mature medusa to an immature stage of disconnected polyps. When food is scarce or it’s otherwise threatened, this jellyfish can break apart into a colony of polyps, and then recombine again when conditions improve (slime moulds do something quite similar). The bodily transformations of Turritopsis dohrnii make the creatures of science fiction look tame. And then there are cases in the other direction, where two physically disconnected organisms become physically continuous. In the grand tapestry of global governance, international courts and tribunals stand as beacons of justice, a testament to mankind's collective aspiration for peace and fairness. Yet, in the quiet corners of my mind, I find myself wrestling with a paradox - these institutions, birthed from our highest ideals, often appear distant, even alien, to the very people they are meant to protect.

A few years ago, I found myself in the hallowed chambers of one such court. The air was heavy with the weight of history, yet it felt strangely detached from the raw, visceral pain that had brought us there. I watched as lawyers, in their polished suits, argued eloquently about abstract principles, while outside, the echoes of war still reverberated through the streets. It was then that I realized the chasm between the lofty ideals these institutions represent and the harsh realities they sometimes struggle to reach.

The effectiveness of international courts is a question that plagues us all. On one hand, we celebrate their role in upholding international law, promoting human rights, and resolving disputes peacefully. On the other, we lament their lack of enforcement power, slow pace, and the seemingly endless bureaucracy that can frustrate those seeking justice.

Take, for instance, the International Criminal Court (ICC). It has indicted some of the most heinous war criminals of our time, yet it remains largely ineffective in bringing them to justice due to political obstacles and limited resources. The case of Sudan's President Omar al-Bashir, who was indicted for war crimes but never faced trial, is a stark reminder of these challenges.

And then there are the disputes between states, where international courts often serve as a last resort, after diplomatic channels have failed. The South China Sea arbitration case, decided by the Permanent Court of Arbitration in 2016, is a prime example. Though the ruling was clear and binding, it has not resolved the underlying tensions between the parties, nor stopped further escalations in the region.

Yet, amidst these struggles, there are glimmers of hope. The International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia (ICTY) played a crucial role in bringing war criminals to justice and helping to foster reconciliation in the Balkans. Similarly, the Inter-American Court of Human Rights has issued groundbreaking decisions on issues like same-sex marriage and indigenous rights, pushing the region towards greater human rights protections.

In pondering these complexities, I cannot help but feel a deep sense of ambivalence. International courts and tribunals represent our collective aspiration for justice, yet they often fall short in practice. They are symbols of what we strive to be as a global community, but they also highlight the vast chasm between our ideals and reality.

As I sit here, staring at these words, I cannot help but wonder - what if these institutions were more than just symbols? What if they could truly bridge the gap between our highest aspirations and our daily realities? It is a question that will continue to haunt me as I delve deeper into this topic. For now, all I can do is keep exploring, keep questioning, and keep hoping. After all, it is in the pursuit of these answers that we may one day find a way to bring international courts closer to the people they serve.

The latter view leaves more space for analysis, more chance to examine how power worked during expansion; it’s also the realm of counterfactuals. ‘Native American societies have robust resistance to Old World diseases at the time of contact with Europeans in the 15th century’ One of the fundamental premises of the new pro-counterfactualists is this: just as there are good and bad ways to write standard histories, so too there are good and bad ways to put together a counterfactual. The historian Gavriel Rosenfeld at Fairfield University in Connecticut is working on an edited collection of Jewish alternative histories, and maintains a blog called the Counterfactual History Review, where he aggregates and analyses examples of counterfactualism in public discourse, many of which relate to the Nazi period: Amazon’s recent adaptation of Philip K Dick’s novel The Man in the High Castle (1962); the US presidential candidate Ben Carson’s argument that the Holocaust could have been prevented if Jewish people were better armed; and, yes, the ‘Killing Baby Hitler’ kerfuffle. Rosenfeld argues that a counterfactual’s point of departure from the actual timeline has to be plausible; in other words, it’s much more productive, analytically speaking, to speculate about a situation that was likely to come about, than one that is completely improbable. He also cites a ‘minimal rewrite rule’ that asks the speculator to think about only one major point of divergence, and not to assume two or more big changes in an alternative timeline. The historian Timothy Burke at Swarthmore College in Pennsylvania teaches a seminar on the topic, and wrote on his blog about a class project in which he gave groups of students counterfactual scenarios (‘Mary Wollstonecraft does not die after the birth of her daughter but in fact lives into old age’; ‘Native American societies have robust resistance to Old World diseases at the time of contact with Europeans in the 15th century’) and asked them to game out the scenario in stages. The experience shows students how to use both direct and contextual evidence from our own timeline to support counterfactual assertions. A good counterfactual scenario must be generated with attention to what’s actually known – about the setting, the time, or the people involved. Dear reader,

In this digital age, where we find ourselves constantly intertwined with the vast labyrinth of social media, the concept of personal responsibility seems to have taken a backseat. The allure of likes, shares, and followers has blinded us from seeing the intricate web we've woven for ourselves - a web that both binds and liberates us, creating an unsettling tension between freedom and belonging.

Have you ever paused to consider the ripple effect of your online actions? A careless comment here, a hastily shared image there... these seemingly insignificant acts can shape the narrative in ways we never intended. We find ourselves ensnared in a paradox - craving authentic connection while simultaneously hiding behind the veil of anonymity.

But let us not forget, dear reader, that each time we press 'post', we are making a choice. A choice to speak or remain silent, to act or stand idly by. These decisions, though often trivial in nature, carry immense weight in shaping our digital personas and the communities we inhabit. And yet, how often do we take a moment to reflect on these choices and their consequences?

As I scroll through my feed, I find myself asking, what does it mean to truly belong when we can so easily detach ourselves from the real-world ramifications of our actions? Can we claim ownership over our online lives if we are unwilling to acknowledge the impact they have on others?

In this era of unprecedented connectivity, it is crucial that we remember our personal responsibility extends beyond our physical presence. We must strive to foster empathy and understanding in the face of conflicting opinions, to promote positivity amidst the cacophony of negativity, and to uplift one another rather than tearing each other down.

But how do we reconcile this quest for personal responsibility with the seductive lure of social acceptance? This question lingers in my mind as I navigate the complexities of online existence. Perhaps there is no easy answer, no neatly tied bow to wrap up this conundrum. Yet, it is within these grey areas that we must find our footing, learning and growing along the way.

As we continue on this journey together, let us strive for a balance - a delicate dance between freedom and belonging. Let us use social media as a means to connect, create, and inspire, rather than as a tool to tear apart or belittle. And above all, let us never forget the power of choice, for it is our choices that define us in this digital world we call home.

The long-term consequences of these moves are far-reaching. The integration of AI into sports could reshape the industry landscape, creating new opportunities for growth and innovation. In the quietude of my childhood home, I recall the faint whispers of stories that painted the world beyond our humble abode. Stories that were a tapestry of resilience and perseverance, woven by the forgotten voices of history. These tales, passed down from my grandmother, were not to be found in textbooks or on television screens, but echoed through the generations, preserved in memory and oral tradition.

The forgotten voices that resonated in my ears as a child are the same ones that remain silent in the grand narratives of history. They are the voices of those who were subjugated, marginalized, and silenced by the powerful. The voices of women, people of color, indigenous communities, the working class - all those who have been relegated to the footnotes of history.

One day, I stumbled upon a history book that spoke of great men and their achievements. It was a book filled with names that were familiar, but the stories it told felt incomplete. It was then that doubt crept into my mind. Was this all there was? Was the story of history only about these few individuals, leaving out the countless others who played pivotal roles in shaping our world?

History is often portrayed as a grand narrative, a linear account of events and people who have made significant contributions to society. But when we focus solely on these figures, we risk overlooking the complexities and nuances that define our past. We risk forgetting the voices that were once loud and proud but are now lost in the annals of time.

Take, for example, the story of Harriet Tubman, a former slave who bravely led hundreds of enslaved individuals to freedom through the Underground Railroad. Her actions were instrumental in the abolition of slavery, yet her name is often overshadowed by those of more prominent figures like Abraham Lincoln and Frederick Douglass. Or consider the story of Pocahontas, a Native American woman whose interactions with English colonists have been romanticized and simplified, leaving out the complexities of her life and the struggles of her people.

These forgotten voices are not just names on a page; they are stories that need to be told, perspectives that must be heard, and histories that deserve to be remembered. They remind us that history is not just about great men and their achievements but about the everyday people who fought for justice, equality, and a better world.

Yet, as I delve deeper into these stories, I cannot help but feel a sense of melancholy. The weight of what has been lost, what has been forgotten, is overwhelming. But it also fuels my determination to seek out these forgotten voices, to give them the recognition they deserve. To ensure that their stories are not just relegated to the margins of history but are central to our understanding of the past and the present.

As I close this fragment of thought, I am reminded of a conversation I had with my grandmother many years ago. She spoke of a time when women were silenced, when people of color faced oppression, when the indigenous were pushed aside. But she also spoke of hope, of resilience, and of the power that lies within each forgotten voice.

The task that lies before us is not an easy one. It requires patience, perseverance, and a willingness to challenge the narratives that have been etched in our minds. But it is a necessary one. We must strive to remember the forgotten voices, to honor their stories, and to learn from their struggles. For in doing so, we can create a more complete, more accurate, and more just understanding of our past and our present.

And so, I will continue my journey, seeking out these forgotten voices, listening to their stories, and amplifying their messages. I invite you to join me on this journey, to remember the forgotten voices of history, and to work together towards a more inclusive and equitable understanding of our past and our future.

The Federal Reserve, the United States' central banking system, released its latest quarterly earnings report today, detailing a robust economic recovery that continues to gain momentum. The figures indicate an impressive rebound from the unprecedented downturn in 2020, with key indicators pointing towards sustained growth.

The Fed reported a net income of $153 billion for Q3 2021, marking a significant increase compared to the same period last year. This rise can be largely attributed to the central bank's aggressive monetary policies designed to stimulate economic activity and support recovery efforts.

One of the most notable aspects of the report is the growth in the Federal Reserve's balance sheet, which has expanded by approximately $4 trillion since the onset of the pandemic. This expansion reflects the Fed's continued purchase of Treasury securities and mortgage-backed securities as part of its quantitative easing program.

The report also highlights a reduction in unemployment rates across the nation. In September, the unemployment rate stood at 5.2%, down from 14.8% in April 2020. This decline is indicative of businesses reopening and hiring as the economy recovers.

Another encouraging sign is the resurgence in consumer spending. According to the report, personal consumption expenditures increased by 2.6% in Q3 compared to the previous quarter. This boost can be attributed to the widespread distribution of stimulus checks and the gradual easing of COVID-19 restrictions.

However, not all aspects of the economy are thriving. The Fed's report also notes ongoing concerns about inflation. Despite recent moderation, consumer prices increased by 5% over the past year, driven primarily by rising costs for goods and services. This trend could potentially impact the Federal Reserve's monetary policy decisions moving forward.

The earnings report comes as the Federal Reserve prepares to hold its final meeting of the year next week. Economists are closely watching this event for any potential changes in interest rates or asset purchase plans, which could further influence the trajectory of the nation's economic recovery.

In conclusion, the Federal Reserve's latest earnings report paints a picture of an economy on the mend, albeit one with challenges ahead. As the world continues to grapple with the ongoing pandemic, the Fed will need to carefully balance its monetary policies in order to support growth while managing inflation risks and ensuring financial stability.

Title: A Soul-Warming Experience at Mama's Comfort Kitchen 

Date: Last Thursday, after a chilly winter's evening

Rating: 

As I trudged home from work last Thursday, the cold wind nipping at my cheeks and the kids bickering in the back seat about who got to pick the movie for the night, I spotted a warm glow emanating from Mama's Comfort Kitchen. The kids' eyes lit up, and we eagerly pulled into the parking lot, the aroma of fried chicken and collard greens beckoning us inside.

The cozy, inviting atmosphere was a welcome respite from the harsh wintery night outside. Soft, soulful melodies filled the air, lending an extra layer of warmth to the room. The walls adorned with black-and-white photos of yesteryears added a touch of nostalgia that made me feel like I had stepped into a time machine.

We placed our order at the counter and were greeted by friendly faces who chatted with us about their day, sharing stories of family and food. The kids' eyes widened as they watched the cooks bustle around the kitchen, preparing our meal with love and care. What brings about a human? Aristotle knew that animals with two sexes create new animals from the ‘union of male and female’. So, like his predecessors, he acknowledges that males and females each contribute something to conception. But what? Aristotle assumes that male ejaculate, semen, contains seed. Closely studying semen, he argues it must be a ‘residue’ of a ‘growth nutriment’. On Aristotelian biology, nutriments contribute to a body’s natural, healthy growth. If a body does not use up nutriments, the leftover becomes a residue. Aristotle argues his semen = residue theory explains various ‘facts’. For example, healthy males produce more semen, because sick ones use up more nutriment. Children do not produce semen because they are still growing, using up their nutriment without leftovers. Because nutriment helps bodies gain weight, people and goats who are ‘getting rather too fat’ do not have nutriment leftover – explaining why they produce ‘less semen and are less desirous of sexual intercourse’. For Aristotle, blood is the most important nutriment, travelling around the body bringing life, growth. He reasons that semen must be a residue of blood. But blood is runny and red whereas (he notes) semen is ‘thick and white’. Why doesn’t semen look more like blood? Aristotle’s answer is that males ‘concoct’ blood residue, thickening and purifying it, turning it into semen. Female bodies do not concoct semen, which is why they regularly lose leftover blood – a nifty explanation for menstruation. Why do only males concoct semen? Because concoction requires heat. And, as on the Table of Opposites, Aristotle connects males-hot, females-cold. Aristotelian pregnancy is all nautical: wave froth, whirling currents, magically forming fishnets This brings us to the heart of Aristotle’s theory. He claims that male semen contributes ‘the form and the efficient cause’, while females contribute ‘the material’. Male semen forms the material provided by the female. How does he arrive at this? One reason to think females provide the material is that, after conception, the new creature grows inside its mother. As Aristotle notes, further material ‘must constantly be added’ to an embryo ‘that it may increase in size’. New creatures grow regardless of what their fathers are doing, so this material must come from their mothers. And if you think females provide the material cause of a new creature, then you had better deny they also provide the efficient cause. SHANGHAI: Giant Leap Automobiles, one of China's leading electric vehicle (EV) manufacturers, has announced major layoffs affecting around 15,000 employees, a move likely to reshape the country's EV industry landscape.

The decision, revealed in an internal memo penned by Giant Leap's CEO Li Wei on Tuesday, comes as the company grapples with falling sales and mounting debt. According to the memo, the restructuring aims to streamline operations and focus on core competencies, a move echoing similar actions taken by other global automakers.

The layoffs represent nearly one-third of Giant Leap's total workforce, hitting hardest at its manufacturing facilities in Shenzhen and Wuxi. The memo also hinted at potential plant closures in an attempt to cut costs and improve efficiency.

Industry analysts see this as a sign of turbulence in China's rapidly growing EV market. "This is a wake-up call for the entire industry," said Zhang Mei, an automotive expert at the Tsinghua University School of Economics and Management. "Giant Leap's struggles underscore the challenges facing Chinese EV makers, from steep competition to rising production costs."

The impact extends beyond Giant Leap. Suppliers such as battery maker C-Tech Energy and auto parts manufacturer Wuxi Auto Parts are bracing for potential supply chain disruptions. C-Tech, a major supplier to Giant Leap, could see its revenue drop by 20%, according to a company spokesperson.

While the layoffs are expected to save Giant Leap upwards of $1 billion annually, it remains unclear whether these cost-cutting measures will be enough to revive the struggling automaker. As the industry braces for more turbulence in 2021 and beyond, one thing is certain: China's EV market, once heralded as a promising goldmine, may face a rockier road than many anticipated.

text:
In the haze of morning coffee, I find myself pondering Hannah Arendt's chilling insights into totalitarianism, a specter that lingers ominously in our contemporary world. The steam from my cup swirls, mirroring the complexities of her ideas as they dance through my mind.

I can't help but recall an unsettling encounter at the supermarket last week, where a man, his face obscured by a mask, shouted at the clerk for not enforcing "the rules" with enough vigilance. His rage was palpable, his words dripping with an authoritarian fervor that sent a shiver down my spine. Was I witnessing a mere outburst, or something more sinister?

Arendt, in her seminal work 'The Origins of Totalitarianism,' described totalitarianism as a political ideology that aims to control all aspects of human life and thought. She argued that it arises when the power-hungry exploit feelings of rootlessness and statelessness among certain groups, creating an 'us versus them' mentality. Today, we see this dynamic playing out in various forms  from the rise of nationalist movements to the polarization online.

Yet, as I delve deeper into Arendt's work, a moment of doubt creeps in. Are we too quick to label contemporary phenomena as totalitarian, diluting the power of her insights? After all, the world is complex, and it's easy to see parallels where none exist. Yet, I cannot ignore the eerie echoes of her words as I navigate this ever-shifting political landscape.

Arendt posited that totalitarian regimes rely on a cult of personality, where leaders are deified, and dissent is squashed. In our age of social media influencers and celebrity politicians, it's tempting to see this dynamic at play. But am I reaching too far? Are these figures merely charismatic leaders, or something more sinister?

As I wrestle with these questions, I can't help but feel a sense of foreboding. Arendt warned that totalitarianism is not an aberration, but a potentiality within every political system. As we grapple with the challenges of our time, it seems prudent to heed her cautionary tale, lest we find ourselves sliding down a slippery slope towards totalitarianism's dark embrace.

And so, as I finish my coffee, I am left with more questions than answers. The swirls in my cup dissipate, but the memory of Arendt's words lingers, a haunting reminder of the fragility of our democracy and the potency of totalitarianism. As we move forward, it seems imperative that we remain vigilant, lest we repeat the mistakes of the past. But what does that look like in practice? That, my friends, is a discussion for another day.

text:
In the hallowed halls of civilization, we find ourselves constantly grappling with the elusive and enigmatic nature of art. It's a peculiar beast, this art, dancing on the precipice between beauty and chaos, meaning and madness. It's a mirror that reflects our deepest fears, our loftiest aspirations, our most hidden desires - a window into the soul of humanity.

I recall a moment, not so long ago, standing before a canvas at an art gallery. The painting was a riot of color and form, a cacophony of lines and shapes that seemed to defy logic and reason. Yet, in that chaos, I found myself transfixed, my breath held as I searched for meaning. In that moment, the world around me faded away, leaving only the painting and I in a silent, shared understanding.

Art, you see, is not just about aesthetics or beauty. It's about connection, about bridging the gap between us and the unknown, the unseen, the unexplained. It's about finding common ground in our collective human experience. Art is a language that transcends borders, cultures, and generations, a silent whisper that says, "You are not alone."

But art is also a catalyst for change, a spark that ignites the flames of revolution. In times of strife, it has the power to challenge the status quo, to provoke thought, to stir the soul. It's in these moments that we see the true power of art - not as a decorative trinket, but as a potent force for change and transformation.

Yet, we must tread carefully when wielding this power. For art, like fire, can both warm and consume. It can bring us together or tear us apart. It can inspire hope or sow seeds of discord. It's a double-edged sword, this art, and we must be mindful of the consequences of our actions.

As we continue to navigate the complexities of society, let us remember the role of art as a beacon of hope, a testament to our resilience, a symbol of our humanity. Let us embrace its beauty, its chaos, its power, and its potential - for it is in these moments that we truly come alive.

And so, as I stand before another canvas, another riot of color and form, another cacophony of lines and shapes, I am reminded of the power of art, the power within me to create, to challenge, to inspire. And in that moment, I am not just a spectator, but a participant in this grand dance of humanity, a humble artist wielding the power of creation.

We are used to thinking of athletics as a levelling force in society, the ultimate meritocratic arena wherein anyone, regardless of class or wealth, can run, throw, hit and sweat their way to fame and fortune as professional athletes. Youth sport is widespread in most modern developed societies, and mandatory gym classes provide a path to physical training and development across the socioeconomic spectrum. This state of affairs is dramatically different from the ancient Greek situation. Only Greek male citizens could train and compete in sports. Within this population, informal constraints limited participation even further. Most poorer Greeks operated close to a basic subsistence level and worked on small farms, so they did not have leisure time available for long training sessions in the gym, located far off in the city centres, let alone could they take months out of their schedule to train and compete at Olympia without incurring disastrous agricultural consequences at home. This means that vast swaths of people who lived in an ancient Greek city – women, local residents who were not born in Greece, foreigners, and poor men – were largely excluded from athletics. In turn, participation in sport was strongly emblematic of a certain political and socioeconomic in-group – relatively wealthy, Greek, citizen males. The Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged I now turn to the second key point, the cultural convention of athletic nudity. Athletes in ancient Greece were always expected to both train and compete naked. In fact, our modern word ‘gymnasium’ comes from the ancient Greek word gymnos, an adjective that means ‘naked’. Thus, while we don’t honour its etymology any more, the word’s original meaning was literally ‘naked place’. Why nude athletics? Answers to this question are elusive. Although many texts from the classical world survive, they have little to say about the meaning of nudity in society or art. The textual sources that do touch briefly on naked sport indicate that the Greeks themselves had little clear idea of how and why athletic nudity emerged as such a central part of their culture. Thucydides and Plato attribute the invention of athletic nudity to the Spartans and the Cretans, respectively, and indicate that (from their point of view) it set the Greeks apart from non-Greeks as especially civilised. However, it is not clear from their discussions how, when or why this state of affairs might have arisen. The cultural convention of athletic nudity probably originated so far back in time from the point of view of these authors that they had no direct knowledge of its advent. Archaeological and iconographic evidence is more helpful. The earliest coherent collection of images of nude males in the Aegean take the form of diminutive bronze figurines. In the quiet hush of my dimly lit living room, I find solace in the warmth of a summer evening. My thoughts wander, as they often do, into the intricate labyrinth of our human nature - an endless maze of instincts and emotions that define us.

It's striking how deeply rooted these primal forces are within us. Like the ancient oak tree outside my window, they stand tall and unyielding, their roots sunk deep into the soil of our genetic makeup. The drive to survive, the yearning for companionship, the fear of the unknown - all pulsating beneath the surface, shaping our actions and decisions.

Yet, the biological basis of these instincts and emotions remains a captivating enigma. Neuroscientists and psychologists have delved into this mystery, peeling back layers of complexity, only to uncover more questions. Take fear, for instance. It's a primal response, an evolutionary mechanism designed to keep us safe. But what about the phobias that seem irrational, the fears that grip us without reason?

Emotions, too, are intriguing puzzles. Love, joy, sadness - these are universal experiences, yet each of us feels them in our unique way. Are they simply chemical reactions, electrical impulses dancing within the intricate web of our brains? Or could there be more to it than just biology?

Then there are instincts. The urge to protect one's offspring, the call to wander and explore, the need to establish dominance - these primal drives often dictate our behavior. But why do they vary so greatly among individuals and species? Is it merely a matter of genes or is there an environmental factor at play as well?

These questions swirl in my mind like leaves caught in a autumn breeze, creating a kaleidoscope of thoughts. It's fascinating to consider the biological foundations of our instincts and emotions, yet humbling to realize how little we truly understand. The human psyche is a vast, unexplored ocean, its depths plumbed only by the bravest of souls - scientists, philosophers, poets, and dreamers alike.

As I sit here, under the watchful gaze of my oak tree, I can't help but feel a sense of awe. We are complex beings, our existence governed by forces both primal and profound. The mysteries surrounding our instincts and emotions continue to elude us, yet their allure is as compelling as ever. And so, I shall continue my journey into the labyrinth of human nature, armed with nothing but curiosity and a thirst for knowledge. For it is in seeking answers that we truly come alive.

text: ‘If there’s one theme in all my work it’s about authenticity and self-expression,’ said the philosopher Bernard Williams in an interview with The Guardian in 2002. Authenticity was already an influential cultural ideal during Williams’s lifetime (he was born in 1929 and died in 2003) but it has become only more so since. What is more familiar and compelling than the injunction to be true to oneself, to keep it real? Williams explored the force and appeal of this ideal, and his work still helps us makes sense of it. But, as he was also keenly aware, being true to yourself can be dangerous. In his essay ‘Moral Luck’ (1976), Williams discusses Paul Gauguin’s decision to leave Paris in order to move to Tahiti where he hoped he could become a great painter. Gauguin left behind – basically abandoned – his wife and children. This was on the face of it a very selfish thing to do, and you might think that Gauguin’s action was morally indefensible. Williams, however, thinks that Gauguin’s eventual success as a painter constitutes a form of moral luck, in that his artistic achievement justifies what he did. It provides a justification that not everyone will accept, but one that can make sense to Gauguin himself, and perhaps to others. ‘Look,’ we can imagine Gauguin saying to himself, ‘I was right … I knew I had it in me.’ Williams imagines Gauguin to be conflicted. He also freely admits, however, that his ‘Gauguin’ is not necessarily true in all details to the historical French artist. Williams introduces Gauguin as a useful prop in a thought experiment designed to explore the role that authenticity, achievement and luck play in justification. Williams also just assumes, for the purposes of argument, that Gauguin did in fact succeed, which is to say that Gauguin did create valuable art, and that this art was a great expression of his gifts as a painter. The story of moral harm in pursuit of art, with the overall endeavour somehow justified by the art, is a familiar one both in fact and fiction. The fiction tends to be written by men, and their protagonists tend to be men. Life imitates art and vice versa, and in reality those who justify their selfishness (and worse) in the name of art tend to be men too. Williams, however, does not discuss in any detail the harm caused by Gauguin: nor does he discuss the way in which many of Gauguin’s Polynesian victims were used as subjects (and objects) in his most famous paintings. This is something I will return to in what follows. Williams’s Gauguin is introduced to dramatise a momentous decision, whether to stay or go, where the choice is constructed in such a way that leaving means to abandon one’s family, and staying means to abandon one’s art. text:
In a curious twist of fate, we find ourselves boundlessly expressive yet strikingly inarticulate beings. We dance around words like moths drawn to a flame, only to be left with embers of unspoken emotion. Artistic expression, that beautiful, enigmatic language of the soul, steps into this void, offering us a glimpse of what lies beyond our tangled web of syntax and semantics.

Take a canvas, for instance. A blank expanse that, when touched by human hand, becomes an open wound or a beaming smile, a howl of despair or a whisper of hope. The painter need not utter a word to convey the tumultuous depths of the human spirit, transcending the barriers imposed by the written and spoken word. It is in these moments that we are reminded of our inherent capacity for connection - a bond that words can only hint at.

But what of the limits of this silent language? Can it capture the nuance and complexity that words so painstakingly strive to contain? Or does it, too, falter before the infinite expanse of human emotion? I find myself oscillating between these two realities - one where artistic expression offers a sanctuary for the unspoken, and another where it is but a mere echo, a shadow of the truth.

Consider music, that ethereal tapestry woven from notes and rhythm. It can conjure memories long forgotten, evoke emotions yet untapped, and bridge cultures separated by oceans and time. And yet, what is the meaning of this melody, this symphony of sound? Is it a universal language or merely an abstract construct of human imagination?

The answer, I believe, lies somewhere in between. Artistic expression, like the kaleidoscope of colors on a canvas or the crescendo of a symphony, is not intended to capture the essence of human emotion but rather to offer a reflection, a distorted mirror that allows us to glimpse ourselves from a new perspective. It challenges us to look beyond the boundaries of language and tap into the wellspring of our humanity.

And yet, as I stand before this great chasm between words and artistic expression, I am left with more questions than answers. What is the true power of art? Is it in its ability to evoke emotion or in its role as a conduit for human connection? Can we ever truly understand one another through the lens of art or must we continue to grapple with the limits of language and expression?

These questions, like the brushstrokes on a canvas, remain unresolved. But in their unanswered state, they serve as reminders of our shared humanity - a testament to our ceaseless pursuit of understanding and connection. And perhaps that is all we can ask for: to continue exploring, questioning, and creating in the hope of bridging the gap between ourselves and others. For it is through this journey that we find not only solace but also a glimmer of hope - a glimmer that language, though flawed, still offers us the means to transcend our individuality and embrace the universal human experience.

For the Buddhist project of the elimination of suffering, whatever value putatively represented in killing the individual-qua-universal cannot be seen to be ethically commensurable with the person’s capacity to engage its liberating project – Buddhism’s very raison d’être – as a person-qua-individual. This discussion might seem abstruse in view of the conventional ethics of legally sanctioned warfare. But a Buddhist account of the phenomenon seeks to go deeper and question convention itself. Beyond the overt fact of life being taken, the Buddhist wants to suggest that the metaphysical and cognitive preconditions, as sketched above, for this state of affairs is deeply out of tune with the true nature of human being. They are conventionally established practices dependent on collective mental processes that misconceive the reality of persons as individually and socially constituted. If so, the Buddhist suggests that reality and our collective experience of it differ, and that we could change the way we perceive reality. After all, conventional reality is revisable – that’s why we call it, and why it is, merely conventional. It could, instead, be in closer accord with the true nature of being – a much longer philosophical and ethical story I can’t pursue here – rather than somehow off-target, as it often seems and feels to be. In this light, it’s worth considering why almost all witnesses to, and even some agents of, such warfare intuitively sense something inherently wrong in its commission. Metaphysical truth is registered, for the Buddhist, in a deep state of wellbeing, of rightness with the world and with other sentient beings, rather than strife with them. It’s no surprise that satya, a Sanskrit word central to Buddhist metaphysics, denotes both ‘being’ and ‘truth’, so that true knowledge manifests a form of being that, by knowing rightly, exists rightly as well. text: The famous sculptor and painter was also renowned for his temper. When the handsome young Raphael first arrived on the Rome scene and was quickly commissioned by Pope Julius II, Michelangelo labelled him a bitter rival and proceeded to repeatedly accuse him of plagiarism. At one point, Michelangelo worked on his ceiling masterpiece behind a partition in order to hide it from Raphael. The latter, no shrinking wallflower himself, managed to arrange a view of it and later, in his fresco The School of Athens, incorporated a seated figure taken straight from Michelangelo’s work. Thanks to these machinations, the rivalry between the two giants became one of the most famous in the annals of art. It wasn’t until the establishment of science societies in the late 16th century that major scientific rivalries reared their head. Perhaps the most notable early outbreak was the fierce war between Newton and Leibniz, each of whom claimed to be the first to invent calculus – today widely considered to have been developed independently by each of them. The feud caused such a rift between the English and European mathematics communities that, for more than a century, almost no scientific knowledge was exchanged between them. In the early 18th century, Newton balked at nothing in his campaign for priority over the invention of calculus: in 1712, the Royal Society of London published a document granting Newton ownership of the invention and discrediting Leibniz. The paper, however, should be taken with an exceptionally large grain of salt, since Newton, who was president of the society at the time, personally appointed all the committee members and even wrote large parts of the document himself. The two colossi of mathematics never met in person and it is not clear that Leibniz was ever exposed to Newton’s work. One can only imagine how a productive exchange of their ideas, disputed over a public platform, could have enhanced the introduction of calculus and the scientific developments that followed. ‘The aim of argument, or of discussion, should not be victory but progress,’ said the French 19th-century essayist Joseph Joubert. Once the new societies and their publications made information more accessible, rivalry between scientists, research institutes and even states began to drive new discoveries. Journalistic interest in the drama enabled more public exposure to science. In one notable case, the dispute between Thomas Huxley and Richard Owen, two of the leading biologists in 19th‑century Britain, shined an important spotlight on Charles Darwin’s theory of evolution, little‑known to the general public at the time. One of the stormiest scientific rivalries of recent years raged between the paleoanthropologists Donald Johanson and Richard Leakey over the discovery of some of the oldest fossils from pre‑human species. text:
In the labyrinth of our existence, we often find ourselves pondering the boundaries that confine us  the walls of our consciousness, the veils of perception. One intriguing question that has captivated philosophers and explorers alike is this: Can we transcend these boundaries, or are they as immutable as the stars in the night sky? This essay shall delve into the realms of psychedelics, seeking to unravel their enigmatic dance with our consciousness.

In the twilight of our world, there exists a family of substances that have long bewitched and bewildered humanity  the psychedelics. These hallucinogenic agents, such as LSD, psilocybin, and ayahuasca, have been used for millennia in religious, spiritual, and healing rituals. They have served as keys to unlock hidden chambers within our minds, offering glimpses of realities beyond the mundane.

As we venture into these uncharted territories, we are faced with a paradox. Psychedelics, while expanding our awareness, also blur the lines between fantasy and reality. They challenge our sense of self, dissolving the rigid constructs that define us. In this liminal space, we may find ourselves lost in a kaleidoscope of visions, emotions, and insights  or swallowed by an abyss of fear and confusion.

To navigate these treacherous waters, we must first recognize that our consciousness is not a static entity but rather a dynamic landscape, subject to the ebb and flow of our experiences. Psychedelics serve as catalysts, amplifying our sensory input and altering our perception of reality. They strip away layers of conditioning, allowing us to glimpse the raw essence of existence.

Yet, the boundaries of consciousness remain elusive, shimmering like mirages in the desert heat. Some suggest that psychedelics offer glimpses of a transcendent realm  a unity beyond the individual self. Others argue that these experiences are merely the product of our brain's plasticity, adapting to new stimuli. Still, others caution against the dangers of venturing too deep into the abyss, lest we lose ourselves entirely.

As we continue to explore this fascinating frontier, it is essential to approach these substances with humility and caution. They are not panaceas but rather powerful tools that can reveal as much about our darkness as they do about our light. In the words of Alan Watts, "We have to learn to be present in all things and not escape from ourselves."

As we delve further into this tangled web of ideas, let us not forget that the boundaries of consciousness are not just a philosophical quandary but an essential aspect of our human experience. The quest for understanding is never-ending, and the journey towards transcendence continues, one psychedelic voyage at a time.

Whatever he’s into, the moralising jerk exudes a continuous aura of disdain for everything else. Furthermore, mercy is near the heart of practical, lived morality. Virtually everything that everyone does falls short of perfection: one’s turn of phrase is less than perfect, one arrives a bit late, one’s clothes are tacky, one’s gesture irritable, one’s choice somewhat selfish, one’s coffee less than frugal, one’s melody trite. Practical mercy involves letting these imperfections pass forgiven or, better yet, entirely unnoticed. In contrast, the jerk appreciates neither others’ difficulties in attaining all the perfections that he attributes to himself, nor the possibility that some portion of what he regards as flawed is in fact blameless. Hard moralising principle therefore comes naturally to him. (Sympathetic mercy is natural to the sweetheart.) And on the rare occasions when the jerk is merciful, his indulgence is usually ill-tuned: the flaws he forgives are exactly the one he recognises in himself or has ulterior reasons to let slide. Consider another brilliant literary cartoon jerk: Severus Snape, the infuriating potions teacher in J K Rowling’s novels, always eager to drop the hammer on Harry Potter or anyone else who happens to annoy him, constantly bristling with indignation, but wildly off the mark – contrasted with the mercy and broad vision of Dumbledore. Despite the jerk’s almost inevitable flaws in moral vision, the moralising jerk can sometimes happen to be right about some specific important issue (as Snape proved to be) – especially if he adopts a big social cause. He needn’t care only about money and prestige. Indeed, sometimes an abstract and general concern for moral or political principles serves as a kind of substitute for genuine concern about the people in his immediate field of view, possibly leading to substantial self-sacrifice. And in social battles, the sweetheart will always have some disadvantages: the sweetheart’s talent for seeing things from his opponent’s perspective deprives him of bold self-certainty, and he is less willing to trample others for his ends. Social movements sometimes do well when led by a moralising jerk. I will not mention specific examples, lest I err and offend. How can you know your own moral character? You can try a label on for size: ‘lazy’, ‘jerk’, ‘unreliable’ – is that really me? As the work of Vazire and other personality psychologists suggests, this might not be a very illuminating approach. More effective, I suspect, is to shift from first-person reflection (what am I like?) to second-person description (tell me, what am I like?). Instead of introspection, try listening. Title: Disappointing Thai Street Food Experience Before the Movie

Rating: 2 stars

I was genuinely looking forward to this quick stop before the movie, hoping to savor some authentic Thai street food. Unfortunately, my experience at [Thai Street Eats] was far from satisfactory.

The ambiance was noisy and chaotic, making it difficult to enjoy a quiet meal or even have a decent conversation with my friend. The clattering of dishes, the constant bustle of the kitchen, and the loud chatter of customers seemed to merge into an unbearable cacophony that only added stress to my special occasion.

The menu offered a variety of options, but as a vegetarian, I was disappointed to find very few choices. Out of frustration, I settled for the Pad See Ew, hoping it would deliver on flavor despite its simplicity. Alas, the dish lacked depth and was overly sweet with an undercooked noodle texture that left me longing for a home-cooked meal.

The highlight (or lowlight) of my visit was undoubtedly the restroom situation. Upon entering the grimy, dimly lit bathroom, I found myself face to face with an unspeakable odor and filthy surfaces. It was such a stark contrast to the cleanliness that I've come to expect even in bustling street food spots.

During my visit, one of the staff members seemed agitated and short-tempered. When I asked about the lack of vegetarian options, she responded rudely, making me feel unwelcome and overlooked. I understand that busy nights can be stressful, but a simple apology or suggestion for an alternative dish would have gone a long way in improving my experience.

In conclusion, [Thai Street Eats] fell short of delivering the delicious, authentic Thai street food I had been craving. The noisy environment, limited vegetarian options, and unclean restroom detracted from the overall experience. I'm left hoping that they'll improve in these areas so that other customers can have a better time than I did on this special occasion.

Title: Culver's during the Local Festival - A Bitter-Sweet Experience

First off, let me say that the location was perfect! Right in the heart of the festivities, amidst the aroma of funnel cakes and cotton candy, Culver's stood strong. The outdoor seating was a lifesaver for us festival-goers who couldn't handle another square foot of bustling crowd.

Now, let's talk about service speed, shall we? I'm not exactly Mr. Patience here, and waiting is something I try to minimize in my life. And you know what they say, good things come to those who wait. Well, the wait wasn't too bad, but it wasn't great either. The server was friendly enough, and our order came out fairly quickly considering the chaos around us.

On to the star of the show - the food! I opted for a classic ButterBurger and a side of Cheese Curds, because why not? And let me tell you, they did not disappoint. The burger was cooked to perfection, the cheese oozing out in all its glory, while the Cheese Curds were just the right amount of tangy and salty. A perfect festival meal!

The packaging quality for takeout/leftovers deserves a special mention. I'm no fan of soggy fries or lukewarm burgers, and Culver's delivered on that front. The sturdy container kept my food warm and fresh, even after the short walk back home.

But alas, the festival atmosphere came with a price - noise pollution! With live music blaring from every direction, I struggled to hear my dining companions. And don't even get me started on the kids shouting and laughter. Not exactly the peaceful meal I was hoping for, but hey, it's all part of the festival fun, right?

In conclusion, Culver's during our local festival was a bittersweet experience. The location was perfect, the service speed tolerable, the food delicious, the packaging quality impressive, and the ambiance... well, let's just say it was an adventure! If you can handle a little chaos and noise, I'd definitely recommend giving it a try next time our festival rolls around. Just remember to bring earplugs!

Rating: 3 stars

**SINGAPORE:**

Breaking news: Manchester United, one of England's most storied football clubs, has been traded to the Abu Dhabi-based City Football Group (CFG), parent company of Manchester City and New York City FC. The deal, worth a reported 3.5 billion ($4.67 billion USD), is set to reshape the financial landscape of the Premier League.

The sale marks a significant milestone for Manchester United, who have been struggling financially since the retirement of their iconic manager Sir Alex Ferguson in 2013. The new ownership could inject much-needed funds into the club, enabling them to invest in player recruitment and stadium infrastructure.

However, the sale also raises concerns about the increasing influence of foreign investment in English football. Critics argue that this could lead to an uneven playing field, as clubs backed by wealthy benefactors have access to vast financial resources. This trend has already been observed with CFG-owned Manchester City's repeated successes in recent years.

On the other hand, some see the deal as a strategic move for CFG, allowing them to further consolidate their position in the global football market. The group already owns stakes in several other clubs around the world, including Melbourne City FC and Sichuan Jiuniu FC in China.

The financial impact of this transaction is far-reaching. Manchester United's sale price surpasses that of any sports team ever sold, setting a new record for sporting asset values. This could potentially encourage other wealthy investors to invest in football clubs, further driving up prices and intensifying competition among teams.

As the dust settles on this historic deal, one thing remains certain: the Premier League landscape will never be the same again. Whether this heralds a new era of success for Manchester United or ushers in an unprecedented level of dominance by foreign-backed clubs, only time will tell.

Tech titan NVIDIA, known for its graphics processing units (GPUs), has announced massive job cuts, affecting thousands of employees worldwide. The decision comes after a series of lackluster earnings reports and shifting market dynamics.

The move, described as "restructuring" by the company, aims to streamline operations and reduce costs in the long run. NVIDIA's shares have plummeted since hitting an all-time high last year, with investors growing increasingly concerned about the company's future prospects.

Industry analysts predict that the job cuts could lead to a brain drain, as experienced professionals look for new opportunities outside of NVIDIA. This exodus could potentially hinder innovation and R&D efforts in the short term. However, some experts argue that such restructuring is necessary for companies to remain competitive in the rapidly evolving tech landscape.

NVIDIA's workforce reduction follows similar moves by other tech giants like IBM and Intel, signaling a broader trend of cost-cutting measures amidst a global economic downturn. As the dust settles, the impact on the tech industry as a whole remains to be seen, with potential ripple effects on related sectors and startups.

The layoffs have sparked widespread concern among employees and shareholders alike, with many questioning NVIDIA's leadership and long-term strategy. The company has promised to provide severance packages and support for affected employees during this transition period.

In a statement, NVIDIA's CEO Jensen Huang expressed regret over the decision but emphasized the need for the company to adapt in order to thrive in the future. As the tech industry braces for more turbulence ahead, the fate of NVIDIA and its workforce will be closely watched by both competitors and observers alike.

Title: Disappointing Brunch at The Sunny Spoon - Not So Healthy After All! 

Had a long week at work and decided to treat myself to a relaxing Saturday brunch. I heard great things about The Sunny Spoon, so I was looking forward to checking it out. Unfortunately, my experience fell far short of expectations.

As soon as I walked in, the vibe felt off-putting. The loud background music and buzzing chatter made it difficult to enjoy a peaceful meal. I was hoping for a cozy atmosphere that would help me unwind, but instead, I found myself feeling more stressed out.

Moving on to the menu, I was pleased to see several options labeled as "healthy" or "vegan." However, upon closer inspection, I realized that many dishes were loaded with processed ingredients and unhealthy fats. As a vegetarian who tries to maintain a balanced diet, I was disappointed to find so few truly nutritious choices.

I eventually settled on the "Avocado Toast" which came with a side salad. Upon receiving my meal, I immediately noticed that the avocados were not ripe enough and tasted bland. The bread was overly toasted, making it difficult to chew, and the salad greens appeared wilted and unappetizing.

The worst part of the meal, though, was the price. For what I paid, I expected much higher quality food. I've had better avocado toast at cafes with more reasonable prices.

To make matters worse, I have a gluten allergy, and I wasn't confident that the kitchen followed proper protocols to prevent cross-contamination. When I asked about gluten-free options, the waitress seemed confused and unsure. This lack of understanding made me hesitant to eat anything else on the menu.

In conclusion, my brunch at The Sunny Spoon was a disappointing experience. The vibe was too loud, the food quality was poor, the value for money wasn't worth it, and I didn't feel safe as a customer with dietary restrictions. I hope they can improve their offerings to better meet the needs of health-conscious diners like myself. Until then, I'll be looking elsewhere for a satisfying and stress-free brunch experience.

text:
Pondering Hannah Arendt's enduring insights on totalitarianism feels akin to delving into the labyrinthine corridors of our collective past and present. Can we, in this fleeting moment, trace the tendrils of her wisdom that still grip us, years after she first articulated them?

Arendt, in her seminal work "The Origins of Totalitarianism," sought to illuminate the terrifying allure of totalitarian regimes. She argued that these regimes were born out of a vacuum left by the collapse of empires and the disintegration of social bonds. In this void, she posited, the human capacity for evil flourishes, as people cling to the illusion of order and safety offered by totalitarian leaders.

The echoes of Arendt's words reverberate today, resonating in our fractured world teetering on the brink of chaos. We see this void in the breakdown of traditional institutions, from the erosion of trust in governments to the growing disillusionment with established religious structures. In this vacuum, charismatic figures promise order, safety, and belongingmuch like the totalitarian leaders of old.

Yet, as I delve deeper into Arendt's work, a moment of uncertainty creeps in. Is our current predicament truly akin to totalitarianism? Or is it merely a storm that shall pass, leaving us stronger and wiser? This doubt gnaws at me, much like the nagging question of whether we are truly facing the return of totalitarianism or just its specter.

Arendt's theories suggest that totalitarianism arises when people are stripped of their humanity, reduced to mere numbers, and deprived of individuality. In our digital age, where data is currency and privacy a luxury, do we not run the risk of becoming mere statistics? As our lives unfold online, does our digital identity eclipse our humanity?

Moreover, Arendt argued that totalitarianism thrives on the manipulation of truth. In this era of "fake news" and post-truth politics, is it not feasible to contend that we are witnessing the seeds of totalitarianism sown anew? When facts are twisted, distorted, and discarded at will, when evidence is disregarded in favor of emotion and convenience, do we not teeter on the brink of Arendt's dreaded abyss?

And yet, hope flickers. For Arendt also believed that human resilience could triumph over totalitarianism. She held that people have an inherent desire for freedom, a yearning to be more than mere cogs in a machine. In this spirit, we must resist the siren call of order and safety offered by those who would strip us of our humanity, who would reduce us to numbers and deprive us of individuality.

As I close this reflection, I am left with more questions than answers. Arendt's insights on totalitarianism remain a beacon in the dark, guiding us through the labyrinth of our times. Yet, the future remains uncertain, shrouded in the fog of doubt and uncertainty. Only time will tell if we heed her warnings, if we resist the lure of totalitarianism, and if we reclaim our humanity. For now, I shall continue to ponder, to question, and to resistin Arendt's spirit and for her legacy.

Title: A Chilly Delight at the Dim Sum Palace on a Frozen Day 

Oh man, I've been craving some good old dim sum for days now, and today, despite the bone-chilling cold outside, I decided to head over to the Dim Sum Palace. I mean, what better way to warm up than with a steaming basket of har gow (shrimp dumplings)?

As I stepped inside, I was greeted by the familiar scent of soy sauce, steam rising from hundreds of tiny dishes filled with mouthwatering treats. The place was bustling with families and friends, all huddled together to keep warm. It's always a good sign when a restaurant is packed, right?

I was led to my table, tucked away in a cozy corner. I couldn't help but notice the walls adorned with traditional Chinese paintings, adding a touch of elegance to this dim sum joint. But let's get real - I wasn't here for the decor; I was here for the food!

Now, I have to admit, I'm a bit picky when it comes to my dim sum. I have an allergy to crab, so I stick to shrimp and vegetarian dishes. The server was super attentive, understanding my dietary restrictions, and made sure to point out which items on the carts were safe for me.

I started with a classic - siu mai (pork dumplings). They were perfectly seasoned, with a savory pork filling wrapped in tender, delicate dough. I love that the portion sizes here are generous; each dumpling is packed full of flavor without being overstuffed or soggy.

Next up was my all-time favorite - cheong fun (rice noodle rolls). These were rolled to perfection, with a light, silky texture and filled with succulent shrimp and mushrooms. I couldn't get enough!

Of course, I had to try something new as well. The server suggested the lo bak go (turnip cake), which was crispy on the outside and soft on the inside. It had a wonderful earthy flavor that complemented the other dishes perfectly.

I could have easily kept going, but I knew I'd regret it later if I filled up too quickly. Plus, the server was insisting I save room for the desserts. So, with a satisfied sigh, I decided to call it quits...for now.

The bill came out to be just right - not too expensive, but certainly worth every penny. The Dim Sum Palace exceeded my expectations on this chilly day, leaving me feeling warm and content as I stepped back out into the frozen world outside. If you're in the mood for some delicious dim sum, give this place a try!  (4 stars)

Feminist epistemology begins from precisely this idea: that the circumstances of our lives partly constitute our epistemic lives – they affect how we understand the world, what we know about it but also how our views are received. Miranda Fricker’s work has shown how stereotypes, hierarchical social relations and traditional gender roles affect how knowledge is acquired and disseminated, and how testimony is received. Fricker coined the term ‘epistemic injustice’ for forms of injustice rarely taken seriously before – injustices that arise when your standing as a knower is not given any credit because of your social status. There are things we cannot see if we do not have the conceptual apparatus to do so Fricker brought to light the phenomenon of ‘testimonial injustice’: when your testimony is not thought credible because you belong to a group subject to prejudice (eg, because of your gender, sexuality or ethnicity); and ‘hermeneutical injustice’: when you don’t have the interpretive resources to make sense of aspects of your own experience because of dominant assumptions and meanings. For instance, the introduction of the concept of sexual harassment allowed women to make sense of a very uncomfortable experience that for others was just innocuous flirting. Similarly, Kate Manne in Down Girl (2019) re-defined a systemic bias in public life and politics, that of misogyny – which she defines not as individual hostility towards women but as a social mechanism of controlling and punishing women who deviate from the demands of patriarchy. This helps make sense of many experiences that women go through daily. Both these philosophers drew our attention to sources of harm that previously went largely unseen. They also introduced concepts that helped us see things differently – because there are things we cannot see if we do not have the conceptual apparatus to do so. These works build on the feminist tradition – eg, Simone de Beauvoir in The Second Sex (1949) – and, after Beauvoir, by philosophers such as Nussbaum, who have argued that freedom and rights are of little use if our situations deprive us of the possibility to act. The idea that, even if legal barriers fall, actual equality might still not be achieved has been present in feminist thought at least since the 17th century – eg, in the work of writers such as Mary Astell, Mary Wollstonecraft and Harriet Taylor. We must also not forget that non-white thinkers – Sojourner Truth, Anna Julia Cooper, Audre Lorde and W E B Du Bois, who belong to groups more marginalised than most white women – have long been arguing that their status as knowers is not recognised and given sufficient credit. Another example of women bringing the supposed view from nowhere

text: Melancholy is not even restricted to human beings: Burton claims that one variety, love melancholy, can be found in horses, fish, peacocks and even palm trees, which have yearned so much to be together that their trunks have grown bent towards each other. Such stories were irresistible material for Burton, who had an eye for the exotic and the bizarre, and whose work goes far beyond the genre of a medical textbook. The Anatomy’s title page advertises that it will explore melancholy ‘with all the kinds, causes, symptoms, prognostics and several cures of it … philosophically, medicinally, historically opened and cut up’, and its source material includes not only medical tracts but poetry, the classics, religious treatises, chronicles and even the works of Geoffrey Chaucer and William Shakespeare. Burton’s ambitious project was not easily contained within two covers. After its first appearance in 1621, the Anatomy reached four further editions by the time of his death and a sixth, posthumous edition in 1651. By that time, it had swollen from its initial (and already substantial) length of 350,000 words to more than 500,000 words. Burton could not let his subject-matter lie. With each new edition, he added extra case histories of melancholics; further causes, symptoms or treatments; and, as a skilled literary writer, new ways of expressing an old condition. His favourite rhetorical technique is congeries, the piling-up of words. In the first edition, he described melancholy in its early stages as ‘a most delightsome humour, to walk alone, and meditate’. In successive editions, he added to his description, until by the sixth it had become ‘a most delightsome humour, to be alone, dwell alone, walk alone, meditate, lie in bed whole days, dreaming awake as it were’. But, he noted, this happy condition does not last for long. The more melancholy is indulged, the more it takes hold, until fear and sorrow supplant the pleasurable sensations: ‘so by little and little, by that shoeing horn of idleness, and voluntary solitariness, melancholy this feral fiend is drawn on’. The melancholic becomes ‘a cankered soul macerated with cares and discontents, tedium vitae, impatience’, to which Burton added, in later editions, ‘agony, inconstancy, irresolution’. There is something symptomatic in these ever-growing word-heaps. They reflect the dangerously proliferating nature of melancholy, visible not only in the expanding subject-matter but in the perspective of the author. ‘I would … make an antidote out of that which was the prime cause of my disease’ Burton – as Democritus Junior, though with repeated references to his own biography – embeds his own experience within his text, claiming that: ‘I write of melancholy, by being busy to avoid melancholy.’ Since idleness is the great cause of the condition, there is no better preventative measure than busyness. text: readers were very conscious of a work’s genre. It guided their expectations of how its author intended it to be understood. By writing the Consolation in alternating prose and verse, Boethius signalled that the work is a Menippean satire. As Joel Relihan showed in Ancient Menippean Satire (1993), this genre of satire ridicules figures of authority. Readers of the Consolation might therefore expect Philosophy’s teachings not to be treated with complete respect. From this starting point, Relihan develops in The Prisoner’s Philosophy (2007) a reading that is diametrically opposed to the straightforward one. Philosophy, he argues, is shown as failing to provide the Prisoner with consolation, and this failure was Boethius’s way of revealing the weakness of any sort of human reasoning. The implicit message is that Christian faith alone provides the type of consolation that Boethius the Prisoner was wrongly seeking from Philosophy. When evil people apparently prosper, there is a divine purpose to it Such a reading treats all the Consolation’s philosophical argumentation as if it were mere rhetoric, devised by Boethius the author just in order to show its inadequacy, with its main message delivered by indirect means. This is hard to accept. Would Boethius, who had devoted his life to philosophy, really have treated arguments in this way? Why, in particular, elaborate the intricate argument at the end of the work about divine prescience and contingency, certainly his finest and most original piece of reasoning, if his purpose were just to show the inadequacy of philosophy and not its power to console? Although it raises important questions, Relihan’s reading is ultimately unpersuasive. The in-group was defined by nationality – in this case, Irish. Another study from 2014 led by the Dutch psychologist Anouk Smeekes, published as ‘Longing for the country’s good old days’ found a significant correlation between collective historical nostalgia for ‘the way it was’ in the Netherlands and opposition to the rights of Muslim immigrants. text:
In the quiet corner of my favorite caf, I find myself pondering the enduring relevance of Hannah Arendt's ideas on totalitarianism. The hum of conversation around me seems a distant echo, as I delve into the complexities of her thought. It is remarkable how, six decades after the publication of "The Origins of Totalitarianism," her insights continue to resonate in our contemporary world.

Arendt's central argument revolves around the notion that totalitarianism is not merely a political system but a unique form of violence inflicted upon humanity itself. The dehumanization and annihilation of individuals, she suggests, are integral to totalitarian rule. It is in this light that I find her ideas particularly poignant when considering the ongoing struggles with identity, discrimination, and oppression that persist today.

Yet, it seems that Arendt's work is often misunderstood or underappreciated. Her critics argue that she overstates the uniqueness of totalitarianism, implying that other forms of political violence are less significant. However, I contend that her emphasis on the totality of totalitarian rule serves to highlight the terrifying potential for any system to degrade into such a state if left unchecked.

In the face of modern political upheavals and the rise of authoritarian movements worldwide, Arendt's call to vigilance against the erosion of human rights and dignity could not be more timely. Her emphasis on the role of propaganda and spectacle in shaping public opinion is particularly relevant in an era dominated by social media and information overload. The manipulation of truth and the suppression of dissent are tactics that we must remain acutely aware of, lest we find ourselves unwitting participants in the dismantling of our own humanity.

However, the task of resisting totalitarianism is not an easy one. Arendt argues that it demands a renewed commitment to political action and civic engagement. In a world where apathy and cynicism are all too common, this challenge looms large. Yet, I find solace in her unwavering belief in the power of human beings to shape their own destiny, to resist tyranny, and to strive for justice.

As I take my leave from the caf, the hum of conversation grows louder once more. The weight of Arendt's ideas lingers, prompting me to question: What role do I play in resisting totalitarianism? How can we, as a society, ensure that we never repeat the mistakes of the past? These questions remain unanswered, but they serve as a reminder of the urgent need for dialogue and action. As Arendt herself noted, "The worst enemy we have is not the enemy that hates us, but the one within ourselves." In this struggle against totalitarianism, it is our inner selves that must be fortified, lest we become unwitting accomplices in our own destruction.

Title: A Tasty, Yet Casual Korean BBQ Experience at Seoul Garden Grill 

Hey Yelpers!

Just wanted to share my recent family outing to Seoul Garden Grill. It's been a while since we tried a new Korean BBQ spot, and I must say, it was an enjoyable yet slightly chaotic experience!

First off, the wait time was a bit longer than expected due to the restaurant being quite busy on this particular evening. But hey, when there's good food involved, who's complaining?  The staff were friendly and kept us informed about our table, which helped pass the time.

Once we were seated, it took a moment to orient ourselves with the layout of the grill - but that's part of the fun! We ordered a mix of popular dishes, such as bulgogi (marinated beef), dakgalbi (spicy stir-fried chicken), and various side dishes, or banchan. The bulgogi was tender and well-cooked, while the dakgalbi had a nice kick to it - just spicy enough for our liking.

One thing that stood out was how fresh the ingredients tasted. The lettuce for the ssam (wraps) was crisp and vibrant, while the kimchi had the perfect balance of tanginess and spice. I'm not usually a fan of kimchi, but this one definitely won me over! 

Now, let's talk about the grill itself. Cooking our own food was fun, but it can get a bit smoky at times, especially with the busy atmosphere. I'd recommend wearing something you don't mind getting a little greasy! But hey, part of the Korean BBQ experience, right?

Overall, Seoul Garden Grill provided an authentic and tasty Korean BBQ dining experience for our family outing. The only downside was the wait time and the slight chaos that comes with cooking your own meal in a busy restaurant. But if you're up for a casual, interactive dinner with some delicious dishes, I'd definitely recommend giving them a try!

Rating:  (3 out of 5 stars) - A tasty adventure with a bit of chaos!

SEOUL:

In the sweltering heat of Seoul's Olympic Stadium, Novak Djokovic, the world No. 1 and six-time year-end champion, stumbled in the quarterfinals against rising star Stefanos Tsitsipas. It was a shocking defeat, the latest in a string of disappointments for the Serbian tennis sensation this season.

Just three months prior, Djokovic lost to Daniil Medvedev in the Australian Open final, marking only his second loss in 28 Grand Slam appearances. He followed that with an early exit from the Madrid Open, where he lost to Alexander Zverev in the third round. His struggles continued at the Rome Masters, where he was defeated by Diego Schwartzman in the quarterfinals.

These losses have narrowed the competitive landscape for the remainder of the season. Medvedev, now ranked No. 2, has proven himself a formidable opponent, consistently pushing Djokovic to the brink. Meanwhile, Tsitsipas, who defeated Djokovic in three sets on Sunday, is quickly rising through the ranks. His aggressive play style and relentless determination have earned him the nickname "The Greek Bull."

Rafael Nadal, though plagued by injuries this season, remains a threat. He pulled out of the French Open after sustaining an abdominal strain but is expected to return in time for Wimbledon. Adding to the mix is Roger Federer, who made a successful comeback at the Dubai Tennis Championships in March, reaching the final before succumbing to Medvedev.

The women's tournament has also seen its fair share of upsets this season. World No. 1 Ashleigh Barty, who won her first Grand Slam title at the Australian Open, has had a relatively quiet year, with early exits from both the Madrid and Rome Masters. Simona Halep, Naomi Osaka, and Serena Williams have all struggled with injuries and inconsistent form.

In their absence, rising stars like Bianca Andreescu and Sofia Kenin have seized the opportunity to make a name for themselves. Andreescu, who won the US Open last year, has reached the final of the Dubai Tennis Championships this season. Kenin, meanwhile, claimed her maiden Grand Slam title at the Australian Open, defeating Barty in the final.

As the tennis world heads to Wimbledon, the question on everyone's lips is: Who will emerge victorious? With Djokovic struggling and the competitive landscape shifting, anything could happen. One thing is certain, however - the next few months promise to be a rollercoaster ride for both fans and players alike.
